issue_title: Failed to build wheel on macOS
issue_body:
Just a minor issue. When installing on macOS, the wheel fails to build. This is due to the missing LICENSE file listed in the `setup.cfg` metadata. Most likely you just need to add the file to the `MANIFEST.in`.

```
  error: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'LICENSE'
  
  ----------------------------------------
  Failed building wheel for dukpy
```

That said, the package does install correctly as a traditional sdist. I don't fully understand the install process here, but it looks like installation attempts to build the wheel first, which fails then falls back to installing the sdist.

http://plim.readthedocs.org/en/latest/related.html lists several related projects, but some of them give 404, and most of them haven't been updated in a long time and may be abandoned. Would you like to prune that list, and for the remaining ones, add a short bit explaining pros and cons / why you would choose one over another?

Context:
I just discovered Plim after first finding PyJade, then hitting syrusakbary/pyjade#70, realizing not having mixins/macros was a deal-breaker, and deciding to look for an alternative. So far Plim looks like the best option, but I wanted to confirm my assumptions. Thanks for your work on Plim and best wishes.

issue_title: How to use flower to monitor more than 1 worker
issue_body:
Currently, I create a Celery worker + Flower monitoring solution based on

https://github.com/itsrifat/flask-celery-docker-scale

Celery worker and Flower monitoring both run in a same directory `flask-celery`

The reason is that, so that Flower has access to Celery worker code module, and the following command with `-A` flag would work

    entrypoint: flower
    command: -A tasks --port=5555 --broker=redis://redis:6379/0

This is how their docker-compose.yml (https://github.com/itsrifat/flask-celery-docker-scale/blob/master/docker-compose.yml) looks like

      worker:
        build:
          context: ./flask-celery
          dockerfile: Dockerfile
        depends_on:
          - redis
      monitor:
        build:
          context: ./flask-celery
          dockerfile: Dockerfile
        ports:
         - "5555:5555"
        entrypoint: flower
        command:  -A tasks --port=5555 --broker=redis://redis:6379/0
        depends_on:
    - redis

Now, I would like to create another new worker `task2.py` code, which will sit inside new directory called `flask-celery2`.

So, how should I modify the `Dockerfile`/`docker-compose.yml` so that flower will be capable to monitoring both `tasks` and `tasks2`?

issue_title: Updating User object with multiple organizations on Enterprise removes organizations
issue_body:
Hi! I found an error this morning, that its quite annoying:

Suppose you've got an user with two or more organizations, and you need to append a tag to the list tags of the user so your code would look something like this:

    USER_ID = 1234
    creds = {...}
    zenpy_client = Zenpy(**creds)
    user = zenpy.users(id=USER_ID)
    user.tags.append("new_tag")    #this is the tag you need to append
    zenpy.users.update(user)          #send the user to zendesk

And the expected result is that your user now has the new tag, but what happens is that the user, loose all his organizations except of one (the default_one).
If you do a requests call (with the requests package of python, for exemple), and the payload you need to send will look like this: {"user": "tags": ["old_tag", "new_tag"]} simulating an append, then you made the PUT method, and know you will see that your user is update successfully and does not loose any organization.

So to implement this with Zenpy, will be like this:
    
    from zenpy.lib.api_objects import User
    ....
    USER_ID = 1234
    creds = {...}
    zenpy_client = Zenpy(**creds)
    user = zenpy.users(id=USER_ID)
    tags = user.tags.append("new_tag")    #this is the tag you need to append
    zenpy.users.update(User(id=user.id, tags=tags))          #send the user to zendesk

This is a current bug, in many zendesk framework, less in zendesk ruby framework (their official), analyzing it, I detect that the framework, just send to an update method, just the modified fields or attributes of the user.

It would be nice to have this feature, @facetoe

issue_title: addict outputs {'__unicode__': {}} rather than empty string when used in django templates.
issue_body:
Here is a rough idea of the code I'm running:

    from addict import Dict
    def get_dictionary(self, orig_dict):
        new_dict = Dict({'default_key':'default_value'})
        if(orig_dict["code"]==0):
            new_dict.other_key = "Double Quoted String"
        elif(orig_dict["code"]==1):
            new_dict.other_key = "Different Double Quoted String"
        return new_dict

and eventually when I use it with Django's `render_to_string("xml_template.xml",dictionary=context_dictionary)` into this template:

    <ParentTag>
        <MyFirstTag>{{new_dict.default_key}}</MyFirstTag>
        <MySecondTag>{{new_dict.other_key}}</MySecondTag>
    </ParentTag>

And it renders as:

    <ParentTag>
        <MyFirstTag>default_value</MyFirstTag>
        <MySecondTag>{'__unicode__': {}</MySecondTag>
    </ParentTag>


I think that (for some reason) addict is returning an empty dictionary, but the output is being mangled. I know the docs say you *can* use `to_dict()` but it implies you don't need to.

Any idea why this happens?

issue_title: fsm.union() with no args fails, on Python 2; causes failing test case in fsm_test.py
issue_body:
Calling `fsm.union()` with no arguments fails on Python 2.7:

    >>> from greenery.fsm import fsm
    >>> fsm.union()
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
    TypeError: unbound method union() must be called with fsm instance as first argument (got nothing instead)   

The method documentation makes it sound like it should succeed.  This succeeds on Python 3.5.

This causes one of the test cases in `fsm_test.py` to fail, on Python 2.7:

        def test_new_set_methods(a, b):
    >   	assert len(fsm.union()) == 0
    E    TypeError: unbound method union() must be called with fsm instance as first argument (got nothing instead)

In contrast, all tests pass for `fsm_test.py` on Python 3.5.  I'm using version 3.0 of the greenery library, as installed via Pip.

issue_body: In 2015, Independence Day occurs on a Saturday, so is observed on the previous Friday, but Calendra currently shows it observed on Monday, July 6.

I am running pylinac on Fedora 26 with KDE with the supplied python 3.6 stack. I've had no problems running it from the python shell (only used WinstonLutz and Starshots so far though), but I've picked up a couple of problems with the GUI.

The first is that py_gui uses os.startfile to display the PDFs. This is unfortunately only available in windows and there is no platform independent way of doing this. A workaround is the following function:

import os, sys, subprocess

    def open_file(self,filename):
        if sys.platform == "win32":
            os.startfile(filename)
        else:
            opener = "open" if sys.platform == "darwin" else "xdg-open"
            subprocess.call([opener, filename])

and replace all instances of os.startfile(fname) with self.open_file(fname)

The second issue is that the GUI is not closing cleanly on pressing the window close (X) button. It does on windows and I seem to recall on an earlier version of Fedora. The solution is to explicitly define the window exit behavior with:
from tkinter import messagebox

    def on_exit():
        if messagebox.askokcancel("Quit", "Do you want to quit?"):
            root.destroy()
            
    root = Tk()
    root.title('Pylinac GUI ' + __version__)
    root.protocol("WM_DELETE_WINDOW", on_exit)

I've modified my py_gui for my use and attached it here.
[py_gui.tar.gz](https://github.com/jrkerns/pylinac/files/1535189/py_gui.tar.gz)
 I've tested it on Windows 7 and Fedora 26. You are welcome to incorporate these changes in future versions of pylinac.

issue_title: Python crashes after jedi imports similar C modules in one Python module
issue_body:
The steps to find this issue is firstly reported [here.](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-python/issues/817)

In summary, [vscode-python](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-python) uses an external Python process and jedi to provide, e.g., function parameter information of Python source files. However, when vscode-python submits to jedi a Python module file (.py) which contains two or more *similar* C modules ```import```s, jedi will import all C modules and then crashes the external Python process. Here is an example file having such issue: [_import_c_extension.py](https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2/blob/master/caffe2/python/_import_c_extension.py)

When vscode-python submits this file *_import_c_extension.py* to jedi, jedi imports both ```from caffe2.python.caffe2_pybind11_state_gpu import *``` and ```from caffe2.python.caffe2_pybind11_state import *``` to the external Python process. However, the C module ```caffe2_pybind11_state_gpu``` has similar objects (e.g., functions) as ```caffe2_pybind11_state```. They differ only in C function implementations: one for CPU only machine, and another for GPU-capable machine. Importing both C modules crashes the external Python process. Actually, I can reproduce the Python crash directly in terminal:
```
[myh@dsrl2 detectron]$ python2
Python 2.7.5 (default, Aug  4 2017, 00:39:18) 
[GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-16)] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> from caffe2.python.caffe2_pybind11_state import *
>>> from caffe2.python.caffe2_pybind11_state_gpu import *
Key already registered.
Offending key: Python.
(crash)
[myh@dsrl2 detectron]$ 
```
This should be not a bug of [_import_c_extension.py](https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2/blob/master/caffe2/python/_import_c_extension.py), because it conditionally imports only one of ```caffe2_pybind11_state_gpu``` and ```caffe2_pybind11_state```. vscode-python developers think the crash is an issue of jedi. See the [discussion.](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-python/issues/817) Isn't it?

Additionally, for reproducing this bug, one might need to build [caffe2](https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2) (with GPU enabled) (commit: 5651c4ce0279c895cbb1d1eeecc6594cc8791103) with GPU support (requiring a CUDA-capable graphics card) from source. Or writing a minimal set of C modules...

A random idea of mine: Add support for using a [XEP-0114 Component](https://xmpp.org/extensions/xep-0114.html) directly. The idea is to have the component connect to an aioxmpp application. The server part of the component interface is emulated inside aioxmpp. For the application, the interface should be identical to a normal client connected to a normal server, except that the server doesn’t do S2S.

Things which need consideration:

* Roster handling
* Presence probe handling
* Handling of reconnect and authentication of the component
* possibly more

The intent is to support other protocols in an application by bundling a Transport Component (such as [biboumi](https://biboumi.louiz.org/)) and have that Transport Component connect to aioxmpp while being able to use it as if the Component was connected to a normal service.

issue_title: adodbapi distinguishing of datetime and date
issue_body:
In MS SQL server, there are two date types: datetime and smalldatetime; datetime has a time component and smalldatetime does not.  in adodbapi, they both come in as basically the same type and adodbapi converts them both to python's datetime.date if the time component is 0 or to datetime.datetime otherwise.  This leads to possible type differences if a datetime column sometimes has a time component of midnight, and sometimes has other times \(for some strange reason datetime.date and datetime.datetime are not comparable\).

I would prefer that sql datetime get converted to python datetime.datetime \(regardless of if the time component\), and sql smalldatetime get converted to python datetime.date \(with the exception that sql null get converted to None regardless\).  
Part of the problem of distinguishing these types is that the type\_code of both datetime and smalldatetime is 135 \(adDBTimeStamp\), however they can be distinguished by the scale field of .description - the scale on datetime is 3, whereas the scale on smalldatetime is 0.

Reported by: airdrik

People may want to trigger renew from another machine in order to avoid the account-key to stay inside the production system.
Renew may be triggered by simply calling `cat account-key.key | ssh renew-cert.py` but for any solution like this to work, acme-tiny must be able to get the the account-key from stdin.
`openssl rsa` already accept `/dev/stdin` as a valid argument but:
- I didn't test `openssl dgst`
- stdin can't be consumed twice or more (once per domain)

That means the program would have to store the value in a variable.
- Does such a request appear legitimate?
- Would you accept a PR for this?
- What diffstat is acceptable/unacceptable?

Let's Encrypt doesn't guarantee the use of a specific intermediate certificate for signing. Therefore it's currently not possible for a user of `acme-tiny` to create a `chain.pem` and `fullchain.pem` without the risk of breaking his setup when Let's Encrypt (suddenly) switches to `Let’s Encrypt Authority X2`. To solve this problem, [a ACME server must send a `Link: <https://example.com/acme/ca-cert>;rel="up";title="issuer"` header](https://letsencrypt.github.io/acme-spec/#rfc.section.6.6.p.9) when [retrieving a signed certificate](https://github.com/diafygi/acme-tiny/blob/fcb7cd6f66e951eeae76ff2f48d8ad3e40da37ef/acme_tiny.py#L156).

A possible solution I can think of is, that `acme-tiny` follows this link, appends all intermediate certificates to the signed certificate and therefore actually outputs/returns a `fullchain.pem`.

issue_title: TypeError for simple object schema with additionalProperties=false
issue_body:
Running validation for a schema like:
```
{
"main": {
              "enum": [
                "duck",
                "chicken",
                "beef"
              ],
              "type": "string",
              "additionalProperties": false,
              "description": "Status of current or last loan."
            }
}
```
will raise
```
...
File "/Users/bartosz/.virtualenvs/django/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flex/validation/common.py", line 384, in validate_object
    schema_validators = construct_schema_validators(schema, context)
  File "/Users/bartosz/.virtualenvs/django/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flex/validation/schema.py", line 230, in construct_schema_validators
    generate_additional_properties_validator(context=context, **schema),
TypeError: generate_additional_properties_validator() takes exactly 2 arguments (1 given)
```

The `generate_additional_properties_validator` is called if `schema.get('additionalProperties') is False` is met and it expects `properties` of the schema to be passed but there is no `properties` in `string` field.

issue_title: Specifying stdout and stderr for multiple Apps
issue_body:
Hello!

Due to the `ClipGlobals` class, the `stdout` and `sterr` can only be specified once for multiple `App`s in the same Python run.

By this, I mean that if you have multiple `App` objects (a different one for each embedded use, etc) the `stdout` and `stderr` are set to value specified by the last instantiated `App`.

For example, in this code:

```Python
first_app = clip.App(stdout=first_stream)

# Later on...
second_app = clip.App(stdout=second_stream)
```

If I were to `clip.echo()` in a command in `first_app`, it would write to `second_stream`.

It may take some significant refactoring to fix this due to the structure of the project. If too large to fix, any workarounds available?

issue_title: Unable to connect to SQL Server named instance - uses Windows authentication without permission?
issue_body:
I'm currently looking into using pyodbc 3.1.1 in a Windows Python 3.5.2 environment to connect to remote SQL Servers (mainly 2012) using standard SQL server credentials (not Windows authentication).

Connecting to a local instance works:

```
pyodbc.connect('DRIVER={SQL Server};SERVER=<local machine>;DATABASE=<database name>;UID=<user>;PWD=<password>')
```

However when I try then to connect to a remote server named instance (e.g. '10.0.0.1\NAME'), the connect call appears to time out and fails with:

```
pyodbc.Error: ('08001', '[08001] [Microsoft][ODBC SQL Server Driver][DBNETLIB]SQL Server does not exist or access denied. (17) (SQLDriverConnect)')
```

With wireshark, I can see an initial UDP packet going out (presumably to the SQL Server Browser to list named instances), but then lots of SMB/SMB2 packets apparently associated with Windows authentication, lots of STATUS_LOGON_FAILUREs etc.

I'm not using 'Integrated SSPI', 'Trusted_Connection' etc, so no Windows authentication should be tried - any idea what is going on?

Thanks

issue_title: Random tests should run in the same relative order when some are added or removed.
issue_body:

issue_title: correct way to start a detached process
issue_body:
Plumbum is great.  But I guess I don't understand how to run a background service.

My program runs a server before it manages a client.
from the commandline, I would type 
> python  path-to-dev_appserver.py   path-to-app.yaml  

with plumbum, I do...

python = local['python']
python( path-to-dev_appserver.py,  path-to-app.yaml ) &NOHUP &BG
print(' I never get here!')

My command does run the server, but I never get to the next line ...

Hey. Django-cleanup seems to break [django-admin-sortable2](https://github.com/jrief/django-admin-sortable2) (or vice versa?)

When reordering some models in the Admin, the AJAX request sent will fail with HTTP 500. I am not quite sure what is happening, but fallback_pre_save (part of django-cleanup) is called at some point, resulting in the following error: 

```
TypeError at /admin/main/trailers/adminsortable2_update/
fallback_pre_save() missing 2 required positional arguments: 'raw' and 'using'
```

Not sure what app actually needs its behaviour fixed, maybe @jrief can jump on the issue too.

`django_polymorphic` adds mandatory fields (named `*_ptr`) to models, but gives them values when the model is created. Hypothesis sees these as normal non-nullable fields, which trigger the relevant health check. However, explicitly providing a value for one of these fields causes an exception to be thrown in the model's constructor.

issue_title: Translating JavaScript source code into other programming languages
issue_body:
Jiphy is an excellent tool for translating Python source code into JavaScript and vice-versa, but it doesn't appear to generate code in other languages yet. I think we could make this translator more versatile since my own [Transpiler-generator](https://github.com/jarble/transpiler-generator) system can translate JavaScript and Java code into Ruby and several other programming languages.

We could make Jiphy compatible with more languages by writing a Python version of translator-generator. Would this be a good approach to this problem?

issue_title: Meaning of "formats" in documentation is unclear
issue_body:
The [documentation for downloading items](https://internetarchive.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#downloading) states the following for the `formats` option:

> __formats__ – (optional) Only return files matching the given formats.

However, it doesn't actually explain what the possible formats *are* or how to discover them. After a bit of experimentation, it turned out that the values here should match the contents of the `<format>` tag in the file metadata (eg. [here](https://download.archive.org/15/items/philosophicaltra9167roya/philosophicaltra9167roya_files.xml)).

Perhaps an explanation about this should be added to the documentation?

issue_title: Wishlist: recursive watching for a file pattern
issue_body:
I want to run a command whenever a filename matching a particular glob pattern (`*.rst`) changes in a directory tree.

I can watch the directory tree recursively with `server.watch('directory/')`, but this triggers for other kinds of files as well.

I can watch for a glob pattern (`*.rst`), but this doesn't support recursive globbing ([yet](https://docs.python.org/dev/whatsnew/3.5.html#glob)).

Possible solutions:
- implement `'**/*.rst'`-style globbing (perhaps backport the feature from Python 3.5?)
- add a `filter` argument so I could say `server.watch('.', filter=lambda fn: fn.endswith('.rst'), func='make html')`

Note that a solution to manually use an external library for file globbing, like the one [suggested in the manual](http://livereload.readthedocs.org/en/latest/#server-watch), is unsuitable because it would not pick up newly-created files or subdirectories.

issue_title: Is aprslib Intended to Generate Valid APRS packets?
issue_body:
Per the [APRS Protocol](http://www.aprs.org/doc/APRS101.PDF):

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/331540/23585999/41e3ae10-0141-11e7-801d-98e3b9b7a516.png)

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/331540/23586000/4e0a057c-0141-11e7-9c90-a7c13a8e249e.png)

However with the following latitude and longitude the resulting string when using `aprslib.packets.PositionReport()` is:

```
3359.7036
11826.9392
2017-03-05 01:13:28,890 - APRS - INFO - KB1LQC-0>KB1LQC-1:!335942.22N/1182656.35En/A=000023Faraday Wireless Node
```

However, NMEA GGA data is sent down as ddMM.mmmm and dddMM.mmmm:
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/331540/23586039/f3046694-0141-11e7-8c89-f53a332cb805.png)

This means that we shouldn't have to do any conversions, just rounding and some zero stuffing to get

latitude = ddMM.mm
Longitude = dddMM.mm

A correct string sent to APRS-IS with non aprslib code with similar coordinates is:
`KB1LQC-2>GPSFDY,QAR,KB1LQC-1,qAS,KB1LQC:!3359.70N/11826.94Wn.../000/A=000037Faraday Wireless Node`

This shows up exactly where I'd expect it to. I cannot get aprslib to generate APRS compliant strings. Is this the intended function?

issue_title: Running SQL on impala throws an error due to commit operation
issue_body:
Hi Catherine, 
well done with this project. I have recently been testing it with Cloudera Impala (using https://github.com/cloudera/impyla) and SQL execution there throws the error:
```DBAPIError: (impala.error.HiveServer2Error) AnalysisException: Syntax error in line 1:
commit```

This is because of the commit operation in the run method. Impala does not support commit:

```
/opt/anaconda2/lib/python2.7/site-packages/ipython_sql-0.3.7.1-py2.7.egg/sql/run.pyc in run(conn, sql, config, user_namespace)
    278                 # mssql has autocommit
    279                 if 'mssql' not in str(conn.dialect):
--> 280                     conn.session.execute('commit')
    281             except sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError:
    282                 pass # not all engines can commit
```

A couple of possible fixes for this that I have tested are:

1. 
Replace ```except sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError:``` with  ```except sqlalchemy.exc.DBAPIError:```
This will trap the execption generated by committing on impala and the query will return correctly.

2. Another possibility is to exclude impala from the commit operation with a direct check/switch. Something like:
```
            try:
                # mssql has autocommit
                if 'mssql' not in str(conn.dialect):
                   if conn.dialect.name <> 'impala':
                      conn.session.execute('commit')
            except sqlalchemy.exc.OperationalError:
                pass # not all engines can commit
```

Additional comments: I am not sure that the commit is necessary or wanted in the run method, so maybe this part of the code can be removed or it can be subject to a configuration parameter (something like autocommit=True|False?
This issue has similarities with issue number #38.

issue_body: Went through the QuickStart. After a successful run of “schema app.py” (with the changes noted in #59), the DialogueFlow console is populated with everything except the user utterances. Once they are typed in manually from the schema into the console, the app works as expected.

I'm building an app for google assistant which uses multiple calendars. I've set up flask-assistant, api.ai, and ngrok and tested it and everything works great. 

The next phase was deploying it to Heroku and updating the webhook inside api.ai's settings to use Heroku's webhook instead of ngrok (https://myappname.herokuapp.com/).

Heroku creates a random port rather than using 5000, so I added: 

`port = int(os.environ.get("PORT", 5000))`
`print("Starting app on port %d" % port)`
`app.run(debug=True, port=port)`

Looking on the logs I see that there is no connection between api.ai and Heroku - the webhook doesn't work.
Is there anything I need to change?
Thanks a lot for this great framework.

First I'd like to say this repository is great, and I can't wait to fully leverage the benefits, as I progress with my project. However, upon successfully implementing token authentication for my programmatic-api, I noticed my [`RotatingFileHandler`](https://github.com/jeff1evesque/machine-learning/blob/e8d38b2c7085d5f4fcfbcaf7d7486ac56260310e/factory.py#L128-L145) no longer logging to my flask logfile.  So, I began by commenting out everything related to `flask_jwt_extended`.

#    # replace default cookie session with server-side redis

    # secret key: used for maintaining flask sessions

#    # set the flask-jwt-extended extension
#    JWTManager(app)

    # local logger: used for this module
    root = general['root']
    LOG_PATH = root + webserver['flask']['log_path']
    HANDLER_LEVEL = application['log_level']

    # flask attributes: accessible across application
    app.config.update(

        CACHE_DB=cache['db'],

        SQL_HOST=sql['host'],

        NOSQL_PASSWORD=nosql['password'],
        LOG_LEVEL=HANDLER_LEVEL,
        FLASK_LOG_PATH=webserver['flask']['log_path'],
        ERROR_LOG_PATH=application['error_log_path'],
        WARNING_LOG_PATH=application['warning_log_path'],
        INFO_LOG_PATH=application['info_log_path'],

        MODEL_TYPE=application['model_type'],
        MAXCOL_ANON=application['dataset']['anonymous']['max_collection'],
        MAXDOC_ANON=application['dataset']['anonymous']['max_document'],
        MAXCOL_AUTH=application['dataset']['authenticated']['max_collection'],

        SALT_LENGTH=crypto['salt_length'],

        PASSWORD_MIN_C=validate_password['password_min_c'],
        PASSWORD_MAX_C=validate_password['password_max_c'],
        USER_ID=0
    )

    # log handler: requires the below logger
    formatter = logging.Formatter(
        "[%(asctime)s] {%(pathname)s:%(lineno)d} "
        "%(levelname)s - %(message)s"
    )
    handler = RotatingFileHandler(
        LOG_PATH,
        maxBytes=10000000,
        backupCount=5
    )
    handler.setLevel(HANDLER_LEVEL)
    handler.setFormatter(formatter)
    app.logger.addHandler(handler)

    # logger: complements the log handler
    log = logging.getLogger('werkzeug')
    log.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
    log.addHandler(handler)

    # return
    return app


def register_teardowns(app):
    @app.teardown_appcontext
    def close_db(error):
        '''

        This function closes the database connection, everytime the app context
        tears down. A teardown can happen because of two reasons: either
        everything went well (the error parameter will be None) or an exception
        happened, in which case the error is passed to the teardown function.

        Note: http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/tutorial/dbcon/

        '''

        if hasattr(g, 'mongodb'):
            g.mongodb.close()

        if hasattr(g, 'mariadb'):
            g.mariadb.close()

```python
'''

This file contains the corresponding views logic. Specifically, the route
decorators are defined, which flask triggers for specific URL's.

@blueprint, organizes the entire application as a series of modules.

    @endpoint, optional attribute defined within (blueprint) route decorators.
        This allows other functions, within the same flask context, to make
        reference of it, via the 'url_for' method:

            @blueprint.route('/example', methods=['POST'], endpoint='sample')

        can be accessed within the same flask context:

            url_for(name.load_data)

'''

import json
from flask import Blueprint, render_template, request, session
from brain.load_data import Load_Data
from brain.converter.settings import Settings
from brain.database.model_type import ModelType
from brain.database.session import Session
from brain.cache.model import Model
from brain.cache.hset import Hset
from brain.validator.password import validate_password
from brain.validator.email import isValidEmail
from brain.database.account import Account
from brain.database.prediction import Prediction
from brain.converter.crypto import hash_pass, verify_pass
from brain.database.entity import Entity
from brain.database.dataset import Collection
#from flask_jwt_extended import (

# local variables
blueprint = Blueprint(

    __name__,

)


@blueprint.route('/', defaults={'path': ''})
@blueprint.route('/<path:path>')
def index(path):

    Note: the following resources can be further reviewed:

          http://flask.pocoo.org/snippets/57/
          https://github.com/pallets/flask/tree/master/examples/flaskr

    '''

    return render_template('index.html')


@blueprint.route('/load-data', methods=['POST'], endpoint='load_data')

    Note: more information on flask-jwt partially protecting routes:

        http://flask-jwt-extended.readthedocs.io/en/latest/optional_endpoints.html

    '''

    if request.method == 'POST':
#        current_user = get_jwt_identity()
        current_user = 'jeff1evesque'

        # programmatic-interface
        if request.get_json():
            # send data to brain
            loader = Load_Data(request.get_json(), current_user)

            else:

            # return response

        # get uploaded form files
        if request.files:

        # web-interface: get submitted form data
        if request.form:
            print(jeff)
            settings = request.form
            sender = Settings(settings, files)
            data_formatted = sender.restructure()

            # send reformatted data to brain
            loader = Load_Data(data_formatted)

            return response


@blueprint.route('/login', methods=['POST'])
def login():
    '''

    This router function attempts to fulfill a login request. During its
    attempt, it returns a json string, with two values:

        - username, user attempting to login
        - integer, codified indicator of login attempt:
            - 0, successful login
            - 1, username does not exist
            - 2, username does not have a password
            - 3, supplied password does not match stored password
            - 4, generic login failure:
                - https://www.owasp.org/index.php/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet

    Note: token authentication is stateless, since it doesn't require anything
        to be queried from the server, to verify the user. The token is setup,
        in such a way, where it is known, if the token is valid or not, and if
        the token has been tampered with.

    Note: more information on basic flask-jwt token authentication:

        http://flask-jwt-extended.readthedocs.io/en/latest/basic_usage.html

    '''

    if request.method == 'POST':
        account = Account()

        # programmatic-interface: implement flask-jwt token
        if request.get_json():
            results = request.get_json()
            username = results['user[login]']
            password = results['user[password]']

            # validate: check username exists
            if (
                account.check_username(username)['result'] and
                account.get_uid(username)['result']
            ):

                # database query: get hashed password, and userid
                hashed_password = account.get_password(username)['result']
                uid = account.get_uid(username)['result']

                # notification: verify hashed password exists
                if hashed_password:

                    # notification: verify password
                    if verify_pass(str(password), hashed_password):
                        # create and serialize uid token
#                        access_token = create_access_token(identity=uid)
#
#                        # return status
#                        return json.dumps({'status': 0, 'access_token': access_token})
                        return json.dumps({'status': 100, 'access_token': 'blah'})

                    # notification: incorrect password
                    else:
                        return json.dumps({'status': 4})
                # notification: user does not have a password
                else:
                    return json.dumps({'status': 4})

            # notification: username does not exist
            else:
                return json.dumps({'status': 4})

        # web-interface: store user session in redis
        elif request.form:
            # local variables
            username = request.form.getlist('user[login]')[0]
            password = request.form.getlist('user[password]')[0]

            # validate: check username exists
            if (
                account.check_username(username)['result'] and
                account.get_uid(username)['result']
            ):

                # database query: get hashed password, and userid
                hashed_password = account.get_password(username)['result']
                uid = account.get_uid(username)['result']

                # notification: verify hashed password exists
                if hashed_password:

                    # notification: verify password
                    if verify_pass(str(password), hashed_password):
                        # set session: uid corresponds to primary key, from the
                        #              user database table, and a unique integer
                        #              representing the username.
                        session['uid'] = uid

                        # return user status
                        if session['uid']:
                            return json.dumps({'status': 0})
                        else:
                            return json.dumps({'status': 4})
                    # notification: incorrect password
                    else:
                        return json.dumps({'status': 4})
                # notification: user does not have a password
                else:
                    return json.dumps({'status': 4})

            # notification: username does not exist
            else:
                return json.dumps({'status': 4})


@blueprint.route('/logout', methods=['GET', 'POST'])
def logout():
    '''

    This route function returns the status of the '/logout' request:

        - 0, indicates successful logout
        - 1, indicates unsuccessful logout

    '''

    if request.method in ['GET', 'POST']:
        # remove session
        session.pop('uid', None)

        # indicate whether user logged out
        if session.get('uid'):
            return json.dumps({'status': 1})
        else:
            return json.dumps({'status': 0})


@blueprint.route('/register', methods=['POST'])
def register():
    '''

    This router function attempts to register a new username. During its
    attempt, it returns a json string, with three possible values:

        - integer, codified indicator of registration attempt:
            - 0, successful account creation
            - 1, password doesn't meet minimum requirements
            - 2, username already exists in the database
            - 3, email already exists in the database
            - 4, internal database errors
        - username, string value of the user
        - email, is returned if the value already exists in the database, or
            the registration process was successful

    '''

    if request.method == 'POST':
        # local variables
        username = request.form.getlist('user[login]')[0]
        email = request.form.getlist('user[email]')[0]
        password = request.form.getlist('user[password]')[0]
        account = Account()

        # validate requirements: one letter, one number, and ten characters.
        if (password and validate_password(password)):

            # validate: unique username
            if (
                username and
                not account.check_username(username)['result']
            ):

                # validate: unique email
                if (
                    email and
                    isValidEmail(email) and
                    not account.check_email(email)['result']
                ):

                    # database query: save username, and password
                    hashed = hash_pass(str(password))
                    result = Account().save_account(
                        username,
                        email,
                        hashed
                    )

                    # notification: attempt to store account
                    if result:
                        return json.dumps({
                            'status': 0,
                            'username': username,
                            'email': email
                        })

                    else:
                        return json.dumps({
                            'status': 4,
                            'username': username,
                        })

                # notification: email already exists
                else:
                    return json.dumps({
                        'status': 3,
                        'username': username,
                        'email': email
                    })

            # notification: account already exists
            else:
                return json.dumps({
                    'status': 2,
                    'username': username
                })

        # notification: password doesn't meet criteria
        else:
            return json.dumps({
                'status': 1,
                'username': username
            })


@blueprint.route('/retrieve-collections', methods=['POST'])
def retrieve_collections():
    '''

    This router function retrieves all collections stored in the database.

    '''

    if request.method == 'POST':
        # get all sessions
        collections = Session().get_all_collections()

        # return all sessions
        if collections['result']:
            return json.dumps(collections['result'])
        else:
            return json.dumps({'error': collections['error']})


@blueprint.route(
    '/retrieve-sv-model',
    methods=['POST'],
    endpoint='retrieve_sv_model'
)
def retrieve_sv_model():
    '''

    The router function retrieves all models stored in the hashed redis cache.

    '''

    if request.method == 'POST':
        # get all models
        svm_list = Model().get_all_titles('svm_model')
        svr_list = Model().get_all_titles('svr_model')
        svm_result = []
        svr_result = []
        error_result = []

        # get svm model(s)
        if svm_list['result']:
            svm_result = svm_list['result']
        elif svm_list['error']:
            error_result.extend(svm_list['error'])

        # get svr model(s)
        if svr_list['result']:
            svr_result = svr_list['result']
        elif svr_list['error']:
            error_result.extend(svr_list['error'])

        # return combined model(s)
        combined_result = svm_result + svr_result
        if combined_result:
            return json.dumps(combined_result)
        elif error_result:
            return json.dumps({'error': error_result})


@blueprint.route(
    '/retrieve-sv-features',
    methods=['POST'],
    endpoint='retrieve_sv_features'
)
def retrieve_sv_features():
    '''

    This router function retrieves the generalized features properties that can
    be expected for any given observation within the supplied dataset.

    @label_list, this value will be a json object, since it was originally
        cached into redis using 'json.dumps'.

    '''

    # get model type
    selected_collection = request.get_json()['selected-collection']
    model_type = ModelType().get_model_type(selected_collection)['result']

    # return all feature labels
    if request.method == 'POST':
        label_list = Hset().uncache(
            model_type + '_feature_labels',
            selected_collection
        )

        if label_list['result']:
            return json.dumps(label_list['result'])
        else:
            return json.dumps({'error': label_list['error']})


@blueprint.route(
    '/retrieve-prediction-titles',
    methods=['POST'],
    endpoint='retrieve_prediction_titles'
)
def retrieve_prediction_titles():
    '''

    This router function retrieves all prediction titles, stored via the
    'save_prediction' router function. During its attempt, it returns a json
    string, with the following value:

        - integer, codified indicator of database query:
            - 0, successful retrieval of prediction titles
            - 1, unsuccessful retrieval of prediction titles
            - 2, improper request submitted
        - string, array of prediction titles

    '''

    if request.method == 'POST':
        # programmatic-interface
        if request.get_json():
            results = request.get_json()
            model_type = results['model_type']

        # web-interface
        elif request.form:
            results = request.form
            args = json.loads(results['args'])
            model_type = args['model_type']

        # retrieve all titles
        else:
            model_type = 'all'

        # query database
        prediction = Prediction()
        response = prediction.get_all_titles(model_type)

        # return results: datetime is not serializable, without 'default'
        #                 string serializer, for incompatible objects.
        #
        if response['status']:
            return json.dumps({
                'status': 0,
                'titles': response['result']
            }, default=str)

        else:
            return json.dumps({'status': 1, 'titles': None})


@blueprint.route(
    '/retrieve-prediction',
    methods=['POST'],
    endpoint='retrieve_prediction'
)
def retrieve_prediction():
    '''

    This router function retrieves a specified prediction parameter.

        - integer, codified indicator of save attempt:
            - 0, successful retrieval of specified prediction parameter
            - 1, unsuccessful retrieval of specified prediction parameter
            - 2, improper request submitted
            - 3, invalid 'model_type'
        - string, prediction parameter

    '''

    if request.method == 'POST':
        # programmatic-interface
        if request.get_json():
            results = request.get_json()
            id_result = results['id_result']

        # web-interface
        elif request.form:
            results = request.form
            id_result = json.loads(results['id_result'])

        # invalid request
        else:
            return json.dumps({'status': 2})

        # query database and return results
        prediction = Prediction()
        result = prediction.get_result(id_result)
        model_type = prediction.get_model_type(id_result)['result']

        if model_type == 'svm':
            classes = prediction.get_value(id_result, model_type, 'class')
            df = prediction.get_value(id_result, model_type, 'decision_function')
            prob = prediction.get_value(id_result, model_type, 'probability')

            if (
                result['status'] and
                classes['status'] and
                df['status'] and
                prob['status']
            ):
                # return results: queried 'decimal' database values, are not
                #                 json serializable, without using the 'default'
                #                 string serializer.
                #
                return json.dumps({
                    'status': 0,
                    'result': result['result'],
                    'classes': classes['result'],
                    'decision_function': df['result'],
                    'probability': prob['result']
                }, default=str)
            else:
                return json.dumps({'status': 1})

        elif model_type == 'svr':
            coefficient = prediction.get_value(id_result, model_type, 'r2')

            if coefficient['status']:
                # return results: queried 'decimal' database values, are not
                #                 json serializable, without using the 'default'
                #                 string serializer.
                #
                return json.dumps({
                    'status': 0,
                    'result': result['result'],
                    'r2': coefficient['result']
                }, default=str)
            else:
                return json.dumps({'status': 1})

        else:
            return json.dumps({'status': 3})


@blueprint.route(
    '/save-prediction',
    methods=['POST'],
    endpoint='save_prediction'
)
def save_prediction():
    '''

    This router function saves the prediction results generated from a computed
    svm or svr prediction session.  During its attempt, it returns a json
    string, with the following value:

        - integer, codified indicator of save attempt:
            - 0, successfully stored the prediction result
            - 1, unsuccessfully stored the prediction result
            - 2, status was not 'valid'
            - 3, improper request submitted

    '''

    if request.method == 'POST':
        # programmatic-interface
        if request.get_json():
            results = request.get_json()
            data = results['data']

        # web-interface: double decoder required, since nested encoding
        elif request.form:
            results = request.form
            data = json.loads(json.loads(results['data']))

        # invalid request
        else:
            return json.dumps({'status': 3})

        # local variables
        status = results['status']
        type = results['model_type']
        title = results['title']

        # save prediction
        if status == 'valid':
            prediction = Prediction()
            result = prediction.save(data, type, title)['result']

            # notification: prediction status
            if result == 0:
                return json.dumps({'status': 0})
            else:
                return json.dumps({'status': 1})

        # notification: status not valid
        else:
            return json.dumps({'status': 2})


@blueprint.route(
    '/collection-count',
    methods=['POST'],
    endpoint='collection_count'
)
def collection_count():
    '''

    This router function retrieves the number of collections, saved by a
    specified user.

    '''

    if request.method == 'POST':
        # local variables

        entity = Entity()

        # programmatic-interface
        if request.get_json():
            r = request.get_json()
            uid = r['uid']
            count = entity.get_collection_count(uid)

        if count and isinstance(count['result'], (int, long)):
            return json.dumps({'count': count['result']})
        else:
            return json.dumps({'count': -1})


@blueprint.route(
    '/document-count',
    methods=['POST'],
    endpoint='document_count'
)
def document_count():
    '''

    This router function retrieves the number of documents in a specified
    collection.

    '''

    if request.method == 'POST':
        # local variables
        count = None
        collection = Collection()

        # programmatic-interface
        if request.get_json():
            r = request.get_json()
            cname = r['collection']
            count = collection.query(cname, 'count_documents')

        if (
            count and
            count['status'] and

@blueprint.route(
    '/remove-collection',
    methods=['POST'],
    endpoint='remove_collection'
)
def remove_collection():
    '''

    This router function removes a collection, with respect to a database type.

    @collection, indicates a nosql implementation
    @entity, indicates a sql database

    '''

    if request.method == 'POST':
        # local variables
        response = None
        entity = Entity()
        collection = Collection()

        # programmatic-interface
        if request.get_json():
            r = request.get_json()
            uid = r['uid']
            type = r['type']
            cname = r['collection']

            if (cname and type == 'collection'):
                payload = {'properties.uid': uid}
                response = collection.query(cname, 'drop_collection', payload)

            elif (type == 'entity'):
                response = entity.remove_entity(uid, cname)

        # lastrowid returned must be greater than 0
        if response and response['result']:
            return json.dumps({'response': response['result']})
        else:
            return json.dumps({'response': response})
```

Commenting out the related `jwt` references, allowed my `RotatingFileHandler` to log syntax errors back into my designated `flask.log`. However, if I uncommented just `from flask_jwt_extended import JWTManager`, from my `factory.py`, then it seems that flask suppresses any syntax errors. from being logged into my defined `flask.log`.  My apologies for such a long traceback. Perhaps I can slice and dice this, by cleverly removing parts of the above code, to determine the conflicts, resulting in the log suppression.

**Note:** I've made some tests (i.e. https://github.com/jeff1evesque/machine-learning/issues/3100), with respect to my project.

issue_title: [Feature Request] - Better Table of Content [TOC] Implementation - Let the User Set Which Header Level Are Included
issue_body:
Hello,

The current Python Markdown supports Table of Content generation as a built in feature.
Yet it is very limited in its customization.

I would like have a different TOC Plug In which enables one more feature - Let the user have the choice which header levels will get into the Table of Content.

For instance have a variable named `activeHeaderLevels` which is a list.  
For `activeHeaderLevels = {2, 3, 5}` only headers of level 2, 3, and 5 (Generated by `##`, `###` and `#####`) will be in the TOC while other will be ignored.

issue_title: C API documentation often doesn't mention returned pointer lifetimes
issue_body:
Take a function from the C API like `MXNDArrayGetShape`, which sets a pointer to shape data of an `NDArray`. 

It is not documented at https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/doxygen/c__api_8h.html#a2035651f4392d249d1b904d5eb0c3406 how long this data lasts, where and how it as allocated, and whether the caller is responsible for freeing it.

By chasing things down to `MXAPIThreadLocalEntry` I see that this shape buffer is thread-local and will last until the next call to either `MXNDArrayGetShape` or `MXSymbolInferShape`. That's an important fact to document to be able to use the API correctly! If this is documented already somewhere, that's good, but then a reference to this section should be included in a doxygen `warn` field of `MXNDArrayGetShape` etc.

issue_title: watchman-make only monitors current and below paths
issue_body:
Running watchman 4.9.0 on Mac OSX 10.13.3 the following occurs:

```bash
$ watchman-make -p x.txt ../y.txt -t trigger
# Relative to /Users/brianmhunt/.../sub-dir
# Changes to files matching x.txt ../y.txt  will execute `make trigger`
# waiting for changes

 ... 

$ touch x.txt
# Execute: `make trigger`

$ touch ../y.txt
(nothing)
```

This is unexpected behaviour and I could not find any documentation on any relative-path restrictions.

This failure occurs also with absolute-paths.

A workaround is to use `--root` but that convolutes the `Makefile`.

If `watchman-make` is not intended to handle paths outside the relative path, then it would be good to document this restriction and ideal to error/warn when one attempts to watch paths that are going to be ignored.

If `watchman-make` is intended to handle paths outside the relative path, then this seems to be a bug.

issue_title: Broken index with many zero (empty) vectors.
issue_body:
We created an annoy index with millions of vectors, many of which were actually zero vectors (vectors of [0.0, 0.0, ...]).
The resulting index returned results which were very far away from the searched vector.
I debugged annoy and found out that many nodes in the search tree had vectors full of "nan" values. Presence of these nodes broke the search algorithm.

Version: reproducible with 1.8.0 and 1.10.0

**STR:** build index from 100000 random vectors + 100 zero vectors (shuffle order). Search for any non-zero vector using get_nns_by_item(i, 10)
**AR:**
  Index is built without errors.
  When searching for most vectors (such as with get_nns_by_item, the searched vector is not even among the results, and they are very far off.
  I debugged the search and many tree nodes were built with vectors of nan value (including some root nodes). Search hits these nodes and the results are garbage.
**ER:**
 either:
    -- raise an exception when building the index and nodes are corrupted
  or:
    -- searching for non-zero vectors works normally
    -- when searching for a vector present in the index, it is always retrieved (the algorithm is supposed to work that way AFAIK)
 
suggestion: perhaps annoy could detect zero vectors when they are added, do not index them and instead store their ids in a separate structure

According to the comments in boto3/s3/transfer.py it is possible for S3 transfers to be throttled based on user-configurable max bandwidth setting:

https://github.com/boto/boto3/blob/develop/boto3/s3/transfer.py#L21

I've been browsing this repo as well as the botocore repo for the last 20 minutes or so but can't find any further reference to any throttling behaviour nor a configuration option for max bandwidth.

Does this functionality exist or was it a planned feature that hasn't been implemented yet? If its the latter, when is this functionality due to be completed? I'm currently doing long-term uploads to S3 and am looking for something to limit the bandwidth use in order to prevent network saturation.

When I try to delete a bucket, I get the following error message:

> botocore.exceptions.ClientError: An error occurred (BucketNotEmpty) when calling the DeleteBucket operation: The bucket you tried to delete is not empty

I know there are objects in the bucket. When I tell boto to delete a bucket, I expect the objects to be deleted too.

I understand there are circumstances where users *might* like this warning. So what I propose is an option for the `client.delete_bucket()` function, like: 

    client.delete_bucket(Bucket='mybucketname',DeleteIfNonEmpty=True)

Where `DeleteIfNonEmpty` is False by default.

Then if `DeleteIfNonEmpty` is True, boto seamlessly deletes the whole bucket, just like deleting a directory in a file browser.

The alternative is for the user to 

* manually list every object in the bucket;
* manually delete every object in the bucket; then
* actually delete the bucket

This is a lot of work for something which takes 2 clicks in the web console. Boto should handle this work for me.

issue_title: REARCH: sset_loop reveals a design problem with argument handling
issue_body:
See the comment made on 6/8 while closing out #29.   Extra code was added to flow_start to safeguard against edge cases that some parameters may not exist in the args object (even though they have default values specified in the argparse boilerplate).  This happens because the argparse parse_args() method is not being called for the respective CMD that sset_loop() is attempting to invoke.

It is not desirable that all CMDs (callable by sset_loop() etc) be bullet-proofed in this manner, because the issue can be avoided entirely by streamlining the way args are parsed & passed to CMDs.  When Tim was still here I'd discussed with him my desire to simplify and generalize the arg parsing, so this issue is just a placeholder for that effort.

Continued from #308

Any possibility this can be moved to MIT?  The ruby plugin is MIT.

If the issue is real world ramification I just had to rebase stuff out of a repo due to finding a worker copied LGPL code in.  I doubt I'm the only person affected by this.

This package works wonder (thanks) by itself but it does things differently from the default django admin site, it becomes a bit of a pain when paired with [django-autocomplete-light](https://github.com/yourlabs/django-autocomplete-light).

Note that in addition of the issues below, yourlabs/django-autocomplete-light#917 will need to be fixed too in order to achieve full compatibility.

### Issue 1: Inserting the row before renaming the attributes

Django [updates the attributes](https://github.com/django/django/blob/94cd8efc50c717cd00244f4b2233f971a53b205e/django/contrib/admin/static/admin/js/inlines.js#L82) before [inserting the row](https://github.com/django/django/blob/94cd8efc50c717cd00244f4b2233f971a53b205e/django/contrib/admin/static/admin/js/inlines.js#L85) but nested admin [inserts the row](https://github.com/theatlantic/django-nested-admin/blob/fdccdce079fc2f4f48dabf950e6fb9c90d488152/nested_admin/static/nested_admin/src/nested-admin/jquery.djangoformset.js#L245) before [updating the attributes](https://github.com/theatlantic/django-nested-admin/blob/fdccdce079fc2f4f48dabf950e6fb9c90d488152/nested_admin/static/nested_admin/src/nested-admin/jquery.djangoformset.js#L252-L254) which means when initializing an item on [DOMNodeInserted](https://github.com/yourlabs/django-autocomplete-light/blob/622b10757bbd0117c6e4b93e6afc68e124e6b764/src/dal/static/autocomplete_light/autocomplete.init.js#L53-L55), it will fail because not all the element is ready.

FWIW, I don't think it would be possible to initialise on the `formset:added` event because this is supposed to work for regular field for which `formset:added` will never be called.

### Issue 2: Not updating the attributes on all elements

Nested admin only replace the attributes on a [subset of elements](https://github.com/theatlantic/django-nested-admin/blob/1dba49177e32f2fddd35f191cc836bd88114ac49/nested_admin/static/nested_admin/src/nested-admin/utils.js#L22) while Django replace the attributes on [every elements](https://github.com/django/django/blob/94cd8efc50c717cd00244f4b2233f971a53b205e/django/contrib/admin/static/admin/js/inlines.js#L81-L82). However autocomplete light [uses a div](https://github.com/yourlabs/django-autocomplete-light/blob/08d69f0938836d511e1866f77286237c6e4f897b/src/dal/widgets.py#L90-L92) for its field configuration and it doesn't get processed.

Based on user feedback, we are looking at improving visibility / ease of use of the publish checkbox. At the moment, it seems to be slotted into the post editor.

I think it may not be a bad idea to add a shorthand to it into the blog menu, to easily publish posts without fishing for the checkbox.

issue_title: Correct white space and imports in settings.py generated by djangocms-installer
issue_body:
The `settings.py` generated by `djangocms-installer` is not very well formed. There are 3 general issues that bother me: **1. The module `os` is imported twice.** The first 3 lines of `settings.py` are: import os gettext = lambda s: s DATA_DIR = os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(__file__)) After that a docstring follows and the following lines: # Build paths inside the project like this: os.path.join(BASE_DIR, ...) import os BASE_DIR = os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))) There is redundancy here. Also there should be preferably no code before the initial docstring. **2. The white space between the lines is irregular and occasionally to big.** After the comment: # Application definition there are 5 lines, then there is another line of code followed by 3 lines. Few lines later there is a comment as a prelude to the database configuration followed by 4 empty lines. The database configuration is instead found toward the end of the file. **3. The indentation of the constant `TEMPLATES` is malformed.** The indentation for `TEMPLATES[0]['OPTIONS']['context_processors']` and `TEMPLATES[0]['OPTIONS']['loaders']` is only 4 spaces (1 tab), where it should be 16 spaces (4 tabs). That should be obvious from the structure of the aforementioned constant. For me it worked to change 2 lines in the function `_build_settings` in the file `__init__.py` in the directory `django`. That is the following block starting on line 263: 

text.append(data.TEMPLATES_1_8.format(
     loaders=(',\n' + (spacer * 4)).join(['\'{0}\''.format(var) for var in vars.TEMPLATE_LOADERS]),
     processors=(',\n' + (spacer * 4)).join(['\'{0}\''.format(var) for var in processors]),
     dirs='os.path.join(BASE_DIR, \'{0}\', \'templates\'),'.format(config_data.project_name)
))
The changes are in bold. However I don't know if this would be a general solution.

issue_title: Wheels built for namespace packages on older setuptools are stuck without robust support
issue_body: Prior to 28.3.0, the `-nspkg.pth` files would mask the PEP-420 Namespace loader. See pypa/pip#1924. With 28.3.0, setuptools now builds those .pth files to disable the functionality on Python 3.3 and later. However, wheels built using older setuptools will already have those .pth files generated in the wheel so will never have the workaround. As a result, all wheels for all namespace packages need to be (re-)built using setuptools 28.3.0 or later in order to include this functionality.

issue_title: Previous median_filter functionality and current float [-1,1] requirement
issue_body:
I just upgraded to 0.11.3 and I'm kind of surprised by the changes in the median filter functionality. I used the older skimage.filter.median_filter rather than the scipy.ndimage.filters.median_filter because I preferred the way it handled missing values. Scipy propagates data gaps (http://docs.astropy.org/en/v0.3/convolution/index.html), while the old skimage median filter would "fill" small gaps. It looks like the old skimage median_filter functionality is no longer available?

My primary inputs are large, single-band float32 or float64 numpy arrays (that often have data gaps).  I initially avoided upgrading skimage because of the lack of support for float arrays.  It looks like this has received some recent attention (https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/1419, https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/issues/1529, https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/1422).  I tried to update my tools with the new function, but then received the `ValueError: Images of type float must be between -1 and 1.`  

It seems that requiring the user to scale their input float array to [-1,1] will be cumbersome. Hopefully this will happen behind the scenes in future releases.

I just watched Michael Droettboom's talk on his excellent and excellently named project, Airspeed Velocity, which tracks a project's benchmarks over time to ensure that benchmarks don't degrade. It seems pretty bloody awesome and I definitely think we should use it, though the logistics will be a bit tricky.

Talk link:
http://youtu.be/OsxJ5O6h8s0

Once we figure out where to run this, we can run 0.7 - 0.10 and then run master or each PR. (As Michael mentions in his talk, the PR stuff needs to be manual after code vetting or it will be a security risk. Running on a VM messes with the benchmarks.) (Obviously it will be a security risk even after vetting, but a much smaller one. =)

issue_title: Timestamp uniqueness by user under heavy load
issue_body:
A DB constraint was added to guarantee uniqueness of timestamps, per user, per collection.

The current version of the ``bump_timestamp()`` SQL function does not resist to *(very very)* load by one user.

For example, running several instances of this script in parallel will raise integrity errors (*~4/10000 when running 3 instances on my devbox*):

```python
import psycopg2
import psycopg2.extensions

conn = psycopg2.connect(dbname='testdb', host='localhost', user='postgres', password='postgres')
cursor = conn.cursor()

nberrors = 0

for i in range(10000):
    try:
        cursor.execute("INSERT INTO records(user_id, resource_name, data) VALUES ('', '', '{}')")
        conn.commit()
        if i % 100:
            print '.',
    except psycopg2.Error as e:
        nberrors += 1
        conn.rollback()
        print e

print nberrors, 'errors'
````

issue_title: Slow for many small string operations?
issue_body:
I've done some tests with Brotli and Zlib

I have an input file with about 30,000 strings (one per line). Each line is between 100-250 characters.

I want to compress these short strings as fast as possible. I use the `BrotliCompressBuffer``function.

With quality 0, text mode, I get about 6 K operations per second. That is, Brotli is giving me about 6000 compressed strings per second. The compression ratio is between 1.1-1.3.

For reference, Zlib (fast mode) gets me 94 K operations per second with a compression ratio between 1.0-1.2.

Is this expected? Brotli seems to be quite a bit slower here. The marginal better compression ratio isn't worth the trade off for me right now.

I have another benchmark where I generate low entropy text strings and counter the bytes going in and out (compressed bytes). With Brotli I get about 21 MiB in and 2 MiB out per second, 5.8 K op/s. Ratio around 11. With Zlib I get about 690 MiB in and 151 MiB out, 190 K op/s. Ratio around 4.5. 

Despite Brotli having a more than 2x compression ratio here it's taking way too long to set up? From the http://www.gstatic.com/b/brotlidocs/brotli-2015-09-22.pdf paper we can see listings of throughput speeds that outclass Zlib. However, I can clearly demonstrate that his is not the case here. I the algorithm old or slow, or does it simply have a higher overhead over Zlib and this is why it takes so much longer to do many small compression operations?

The resulting objects take a `geocoder` object and uses the geopy api (namely calling a `geocode` method on the `geocoder` and passing in an address from the given list).

I have a problem with the following Python script, which performs a SPARQL update query.

Basically, I want to alter a set of triples : some of them must be plainly deleted (call them D), some of them must be updated (call them U).

[test-update.py.txt](https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/files/264936/test-update.py.txt)

As can be seen in the script, I use a SPARUL query combining DELETE, INSERT, and a WHERE with an OPTIONAL. The idea is that all concerned triples (D+U) match the "mandatory" part of the WHERE, while only the U triples match the OPTIONAL ; and the DELETE clause only uses variables of the "mandatory" part, while the INSERT clause uses a variable from the OPTIONAL clause.

My problem is that the SPARUL raises an exception (it produces an unexpected None value).

Strangely, the equivalent SELECT query works.

issue_title: Support socket connections for local patroni instance
issue_body:
Currently, the local patroni instance can only connect using TCP.  We should support local unix socket connections as well; that's both faster and more secure.

This will require some replumbing, which I regard as a cleanup anyway.  Currently, we read config information, use that to create a URL, then parse that URL to get the config information back.  This seems unnecessarily ornate; we should either use URLs in the config in the first place, or we should avoid this whole unnecessary step and use the config fields directly.  For backwards-compatibility reasons, I plan to use the latter path.

I'll also use a file path for the socket interface if that's preferred by the user.  It should  be simple to differentiate this from an IPv4/v6 address.

I plan to do this in the next couple of days, so let me know if you have comments on the design.

### Description
Application runs fine when client is on same computer and access is done using "localhost" on the URL. When client is on a remote computer OR if the client is on the same computer but access is done using "127.0.0.1" the application returns the 404 message below for the previously running url paths (including /ui)

```
{
  "detail": "The requested URL was not found on the server.  If you entered the URL manually please check your spelling and try again.",
  "status": 404,
  "title": "Not Found",
  "type": "about:blank"
}
```
I've tested using both app.run(host='0.0.0.0') and app.run(host='127.0.0.1'). 

I'm using flask.

I'm running using "python app.py".

Any help would be appreciated.

Thanks

I have an unusual issue that might be particular to my use case.

I am comparing the data emitted from kafka and pubsub. Starting from 0.37 release, when an error occurred (log below), the stream from pubsub seems to be throttled and spiky.

I noticed there's an error at the time of divergence between the two. This error also happened in 0.35.4 but it did not cause the divergence

Here's a snippet for my code
```python 
while self._running:

```

issue_body:
Using the templates found [here](http://gcloud-python.readthedocs.io/en/stable/bigquery-table.html) 
Running the commands

```python
job = client.load_table_from_storage(
    'load-from-storage' + datetime.now().strftime('%Y%m%d%H%M'),
    table, gsbucket)
job.skip_leading_rows = 0
job.writeDisposition = 'WRITE_TRUNCATE'
job.field_delimiter = ','
job.begin()
```

This piece of code appends to my table instead of overwriting it like the [docs](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/v2/jobs) say.
> WRITE_TRUNCATE: If the table already exists, BigQuery overwrites the table data. 

I know this because if I use:

```python
table.reload()
print(table.num_rows)
```

after the job it has increased by 2 million - the size of the table.

I use a workaround as follows:

```python
if disposition == 'WRITE_TRUNCATE':
    schema = table.schema
    while table.exists():
        table.delete()
    table = dataset.table(table_name, schema=schema)
    table.create()
```
Which seems to work fine, and the while loop is just me making certain that the table is deleted but when I tested it without the while loop it also worked.

Anyway, the issue is WRITE_TRUNCATE isn't doing what it say it does in the docs.

issue_title: antlr3 generated target has unstable hash and uncache-able due to timestamp in generated code
issue_body:
antlr3 puts a comment with timestamp at the top of generated java sources, causing the target artifact to have an ever-changing fp which becomes un-cacheable. Any targets dependent on an antlr target will also be uncacheable due to ever-changing dependent hashes, causing build slowdown. Proposed fix is either

1. scrub the generated sources to remove timestamps
2. change fingerprint strategy for java_antlr_library so that it hashes the original .g source rather than the generated

issue_title: Expose a way to query whether a particular goal is clean/dirty
issue_body:
The `build_invalidator` stores a mapping from target id to target fingerprint, and so given a target and a fingerprint strategy, it can answer queries about whether a target needs to be rebuilt.

It would be useful to expose this as a task/goal for the intellij pants plugin to consume. Currently, the `./pants invalidate` goal purges this information, but nothing exposes it for a user... part of the reason for that might be the requirement to have the relevant instance of the task, and the fingerprint strategy for the class.

In the new engine this should get easier, because the query will simply be "is this root node currently valid"? cc @kwlzn

When a custom filter is not available, Dactyl tries loading it from all the custom filter paths, then falls back to the built-in filter path. Import errors on the last step aren't caught & can't be bypassed, but any case of a missing or inaccurate filter falls through to that case.

This gives a misleading error in a lot of cases and interferes with some other things too.

The way I read the documentation, --devices-ids -20 in conjunction with --use-cpu should make my translation run use 20 CPUs.

Note that my installation is through conda and it is cu80 based.  Should I create a separate conda environment with mxnet-mkl and `pip install sockeye`?

issue_title: Keras-MXNet custom loss with slice operation loses shape information
issue_body:
If you are using a Custom Loss function and if you use a slice operation in custom loss function, you need to explicitly set the shape of the loss tensor before returning from your custom loss function.

Example:

```
# Your custom loss function.
def my_custom_loss(y, y_hat):
    # Assume below shapes for demonstrating the issue
    # y.shape -> (None, 10)
    # y_hat.shape -> (None, 10) 
    
    y = y[:, :8]
    y_hat = y_hat[:, :8]
   
   # You used a slice operation. Shape information of y and y_hat is lost after this operation.
   # y.shape -> ()
   # y_hat.shape -> ()

    loss = y - y_hat
    
    # loss.shape -> ()  => This causes custom loss calculation to fail with shape mismatch errors.

    ##### This is a workaround required for MXNet backend due to issue in MXNet - https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/11349 #####
   # Explicitly set the shape of loss tensor before returning. Since first dimension is batch_size you can leave it as 'None' i.e., unknown.
    loss._keras_shape = (None, 8)  
    return (loss)
```

# Reason

As described in this issue https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/11349 MXNet has a strange behavior for slice operator where infer_partial_shape() call returns pre-maturely even if one of the dimension of input is None (unknown). Since y, y_hat batch size is unknown in our case in Keras, after slicing, shape info is lost.

# Solution

Working with MXNet team to resolve the issue and allow infer_shape_partial() to try to come up with shape information.

issue_body:
The docs say that the fields should be

`Required S3 URI to the Swagger document describing the API.`

Yet the example is of a local file:

issue_title: Resources models always use default handler by default
issue_body:
Hello!

In our project, we have next settings for `handler` (_custom_ handler):
```
handler = APIRequest(sandbox=False,  timeout=300)
```
We found that resources models (e.g. `Document`) ignore _custom_ handler and use default handler instead:
```
>>> handler.timeout
300

>>> Document().handler.timeout
30.0
```

Setting of _custom_ handler will be used only in case we will pass _custom_ handler explicitly to the resource model:
```
>>> Document(handler=handler).handler.timeout
300
```

In docs I cannot find information that we should pass _custom_ handler to each resource we create. Probably this should be fixed or at least indicated in docs.

issue_title: Support tar file in load_image API

Maybe load_image API can support it.

issue_title: "error: unrecognized arguments" when running prediction tutorial
issue_body:
Hi, in response to #884 , I am trying to get this tutorial running: https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/tutorials/getting_started/making_predictions_and_creating_a_demo.md
But when I run this command in the tutorial:
```
python -m allennlp.run predict \
    tests/fixtures/model.tar.gz \
    tests/fixtures/s2_papers.jsonl \
    --include-package my_library \
    --predictor paper-classifier
```

I encounter this error:
`error: unrecognized arguments: --include-package my_library --predictor paper-classifier`

After looking at allennlp's `run.py` (https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/allennlp/run.py)
I find it calls `python -m allennlp.run`, which I presume is still currently at the 0.3 version? Does that mean I need to wait until I can download v0.4 from pip, or is there a quick fix available?

@cristipp wrote his own run script, in part so he could use jsonnet for his config files:

https://jsonnet.org/

jsonnet is a much more featured (and probably better supported) configuration language than HOCON. Insofar as our "HOCON" files are all "JSON with comments", they should be valid jsonnet as well. (If someone is using more advanced HOCON features, they might break, although I don't think I know of anyone doing that.)

jsonnet is a little involved, but it allows functions and inheritance, and it would make it simpler to switch between e.g. local and GPU-enabled experiments. it's also easy to imagine that it could make it a lot simpler to do hyperparameter searching. (I haven't fully thought through this.)

When I try to run `from allennlp.commands.serve import DEFAULT_MODELS` (from [this test](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/master/tests/models/sniff_test.py#L3)), I get the error:

As a tabulator-py user I want to find and replace strings in my data, using one simple parameter, so that I don't need to write a post-processor script.

## dataset examples where we need a replace ability
https://github.com/datasets/cash-surplus-deficit/blob/master/scripts/process.py [lines 27,28]
