### replace format
* I will use a dictionary to pass agruments into the `Stream` constructor, so later we could extend 'replace' with more keywords:  
`replace={'old': 'q1', 'new': '-03-31'}`
* For several replacements we could use a list of dictionaries: 
```python
replace=[
    {'old': 'q1', 'new': '-03-31'},
    {'old': 'q2', 'new': '-06-30'}
]
```

* [ ] [+2h] To enable RegExp we could pass `'regex': True`. Here's a real example from the script, that need an automation: `replace('\.|"|,|\'|:|-|\(|\)', '', regex=True)`. 
In such case the parameter will look like that:
```python
replace={
    'old': '\.|"|,|\'|:|-|\(|\)',
    'new': '',
    'regex': True
}
```

* [ ] [+2h] We could specify column to apply replace function to. (e.g we need to replace 'q1' to '-03-31' but there could be 'q1' in other columns, that we does not want to break)
```python
replace={'old': 'q1',
         'new': '-03-31',
         'regex': True,
        'column': N or 'name'}
```


## Tasks

* [x] analyze [30m]
* [x] learn tabulator-py docs, run examples [20m]
* [x] find and analyse the 'Stream' commands parser [20m]
* [x] write tests for a new processor`replace: "old", "new"` [20m]
* [ ] create the function [50m]
* [ ] check all the old and new tests are working [10m]
* [ ] docs: update 'Stream commands' and create a 'replace' section [25m]
* [ ] do post-review fixes [20m]
* [ ] PR to main repo [20m]
* [ ] fix the PR post-review fixes, if any [0..20m]
Overall [4 to 4.5h]

Original: https://github.com/AcckiyGerman/tabulator-py/issues/2

We should add a tempest-plugin to our repo, so we can start running additional tests in the devstack framework beyond the community ones:

http://docs.openstack.org/developer/tempest/plugin.html

issue_title: Create a config variable to set the number of Docker images to keep for an app
issue_body:
Running and pushing many different updates to a Deis environment can add up to a lot of Docker images.  I have no reason for keep ALL Docker images for an app along the way.   

It would be nice if we could add a config var like DIMAGE_CNT=3 that would force the builder to always remove any Docker images for an application that are not the most recent 3 images.

The goal is to prevent 100 different images from accumulating that haven't been used in weeks and are just consuming disk space.

issue_title: ls --long: / at end of directory names
issue_body: Without --long, globus ls puts a / at the end of directory names.  But with --long, it leaves the / off the end of directory names.  We should consistently include a / at the end of directory names.

issue_title: Remove owner information in endpoint search and endpoint show?
issue_body:
When doing `endpoint search` (or `endpoint show`) in text mode, the "Owner" column (or row) is listed and prominent, but as far as I know, this is something we are to be de-emphasizing and eventually removing and instead pointing people to look at which identities/groups have the administrator role on the endpoint.

Is there another field (or are there other fields) that we should consider showing instead here? (e.g. `contact_email`, some sort of "type" field based on presence of `host_endpoint_id` or `is_globus_connect` flag)

issue_title: customized `cd` with output breaks sourced activate/deactivate
issue_body:
In bash on osx I have a customized `cd` like:

```
function cd() {
    builtin cd "$@" || return;

    # Need -w with pipe for 78 sized screen
    ls -CFh -w $COLUMNS | head -n 25;
}
```

Since activate and deactivate are sourced, they will be in scope for this cd, causing the following errors:

```
$ source activate snowflakes
discarding /Users/$USER/anaconda/envs/snowflakes/bin from PATH
prepending /Users/$USER/anaconda/envs/snowflakes/bin to PATH
ls: 115: No such file or directory

$ source deactivate 
ls: 115: No such file or directory
discarding /Users/$USER/anaconda/envs/snowflakes/bin from PATH
```

The relevant lines are:

```
get_dirname() {
    echo "$(cd "$(dirname "$1")" && pwd)"
}
...
    _DIR="$( cd -P "$(dirname $_ABS_SCRIPT_LOCATION)" && pwd )"
```

This is low priority, but should also be easy to fix, perhaps just using `builtin cd` instead.
Manually replacing the `builtin` resolved the issue for me.

Thanks

issue_title: Old repos (repodata) made with index can't be read by conda
issue_body:
Anyone with an old repodata created with `conda index` before some period of time are unreadable by conda.  Some people have production repos that they don't upgrade and conda fails in non-obvious ways.  I'm not sure if the "bad" metadata needs to come from a package is a transitive dependency, but in some cases there is no error: "bad metadata".  If you try to install the package by itself, conda will finally give you a generic "bad metadata" error, but there is no indication of how to solve this problem. 

Depending on what "bad metadata" is caused by, conda could advise re-indexing the repodata.  Either way, the repodata format should be versioned to prevent more hidden errors like this.

`conda.gateways.logging.initialize_logging` calls `initialize_root_logger` and `initialize_conda_logger` which add custom handlers named "stderr" to the root and "conda" logger.
`initialize_logging` gets called when importing `conda.gateways`, `conda.gateways.logging`  and `conda.cli.python_api`.

This is problematic when `conda.cli.python_api.run_command` is incorporated in a program which also uses `logging` (e.g., defining custom formatters, etc.). See:
```python
# uses separate processes to re-init `logging` via `basicConfig`
from multiprocessing import Process


def run(level):
    from logging import basicConfig, getLogger
    awesome_format = "\n>>> AWESOME %(levelname)s: %(message)s"
    basicConfig(format=awesome_format, level=level)
    logger = getLogger(__file__)
    msg = "some message"

    def log():
        logger.debug(msg)
        logger.info(msg)
        logger.warn(msg)
        logger.error(msg)

    log()
    import conda.cli.python_api
    #import conda.gateways
    #import conda.gateways.logging
    log()


for level in ("INFO", "WARN", "ERROR"):
    print("{:-^60}".format(level))
    process = Process(target=run, args=(level,))
    process.start()
    process.join()
    print("\n\n")
```
Output (notice the duplicate `ERROR` messages (with `conda`'s logging format) and omitted `WARNING`s):
```
----------------------------INFO----------------------------

>>> AWESOME INFO: some message

>>> AWESOME WARNING: some message

>>> AWESOME ERROR: some message

>>> AWESOME INFO: No cio_test package found.

>>> AWESOME ERROR: some message
ERROR cli_python_api_logging.py:log(16): some message



----------------------------WARN----------------------------

>>> AWESOME WARNING: some message

>>> AWESOME ERROR: some message

>>> AWESOME ERROR: some message
ERROR cli_python_api_logging.py:log(16): some message



---------------------------ERROR----------------------------

>>> AWESOME ERROR: some message

>>> AWESOME ERROR: some message
ERROR cli_python_api_logging.py:log(16): some message



```

Attempting to install `trinity==2.4.0` results in `bowtie=2.2.5=0` being installed. This is not the lastest bowtie2 build (there is a build 2) and it doesn't work. Also, attempting to install `bowtie==2.2.5` results in the broken build 0 install. However, first installing `bowtie=2.2.5_py36_2` does work and subsequently installing `trinity==2.4.0` into that envrionment results in `bowtie2 2.3.3.1-py36pl5.22.0_0` being installed. Then everything works.

From a discussion on Gitter with @mbargull :
> Sounds like Conda's solver didn't prioritize its objectives (maximize req. pkg. version, maximize req. pkg. build number, minimize number of packages) correctly. The cause seems to be the dependency on pythonfor the newer builds which requires more packages to be installed. If you explicitly add python to the list of packages of your conda install/create command, you'll see it will then pick up the newer build. Interestingly conda 4.4.0rc1 behaves correctly.

We've had some issue with GitFlow with overwritten commits etc. This seems to be a common problem with this system. Perhaps we should discuss this :-)

Something simpler in any case!

References:
- http://endoflineblog.com/gitflow-considered-harmful
- https://about.gitlab.com/2014/09/29/gitlab-flow/

[Count Filter](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/4.1/filtertype_count.html) doesn't seem to be working as expected in Curator **4.1**, given this configuration:

```
actions:
  1:
    action: delete_snapshots
    description: >-
      Delete all snapshots other than latest 5 (based on name) for backup
      snapshots. 
    options:
      repository: 'my_backups'
      timeout_override:
      continue_if_exception: False
      disable_action: False
    filters:
    - filtertype: pattern
      kind: prefix
      value: 'curator-'
    - filtertype: count
      count: 5
      reverse: True
```

Results in:

```
2016-09-23 10:42:50,007 ERROR     Failed to complete action: delete_snapshots.  <type 'exceptions.KeyError'>: 'count'
```
The implementation of put_script sends the request to /_scripts/<lang>/<id>, when it should send it to /_scripts/<id> according to ES documentation.

Regarding the documentation found here:
http://elasticsearch-py.readthedocs.io/en/1.9.0/api.html#elasticsearch.Elasticsearch.search

The `.search()` method argument, `from`, should be `from_`. As seen here: https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blob/1.9.0/elasticsearch/client/__init__.py#L449

issue_title: az vm create under PowerShell won't permit empty public-ip-address
issue_body:
### Description
**Outline the issue here:**
I first noticed this issue with my full az vm create command, and I progressively simplified it until it was basically just `az vm create --resource-group TEST --name TEST-VNAS --public-ip-address ""` and it still threw the error: `az vm create: error: argument --public-ip-address: expected one argument`. That's when I finally thought maybe this is just an issue with az under PowerShell. So I tried my original full command under a simple Command Prompt window, and it worked fine.

issue_title: does az storage blob download take advantage of sparse files? It should..
issue_body:
Either I cannot find out how to do it, or `az storage blob download` does not seem to take advantage of sparse files.
Downloading a vm os disk snapshot of 30GB, containing only about 1.7GB of data takes more than 60 minutes while [azcopy](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-use-azcopy-linux) downloads the same file in 7 minutes.

The only reference to something like this in the documentation is `--max-connections`:

```
    --max-connections             :  ...
                                    ... 
                                    This may also be useful if many blobs are expected to be empty
                                    as an extra request is required for empty blobs if
                                    max_connections is greater than 1.  Default: 2.
```

However, setting max-connections to 1 does not seem to make a difference to using the default of 2.

Am I doing something wrong?
---

### Environment summary

Tried in two different environments, mac os:
```
$ echo $SHELL
zsh
$ brew install azure-cli
...

issue_title: Unable to debug C# Functions in VS2017 Preview after clean install
issue_body:
### Description
After having to repave my machine, I tried to re-install my Azure Functions tooling. The order of the install was:

- VS2017 Preview (15.3)
- Node.js/NPM
- Azure Functions Extension for VS2017 Preview
- Azure Functions Core Tools (via NPM)

Tried to debug a C# Function but I got the following error:
![functionerror](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4126750/27604845-8a062548-5b72-11e7-86be-3a5aa9cee7f0.png)

I then installed the **Azure Function CLI (old version)** which failed to install due to bandwidth issues. To fix this I download it manually following the steps outlined here: [https://github.com/Azure/Azure-Functions/issues/110](https://github.com/Azure/Azure-Functions/issues/110). 

I unzipped the files in the following location:
`C:\Users\<userprofile>\AppData\Local\Azure.Functions.Cli\1.0.0-beta.9`

This made VS2017 to stop complaining, but the Function failed to run since there was a mismatch between the CLI tools and the latest Functions project format (WebJobs based). 

###Workaround
To make it work, I went in my NPM package folder and copied the executable and accompanying dlls to the directory that VS2017 expects to find them. To make it more explicit, I copied from here:

**C:\Users\<userprofile>\AppData\Roaming\npm\node_modules\azure-functions-core-tools\bin**
to
**C:\Users\<userprofile>\AppData\Local\Azure.Functions.Cli\1.0.0-beta.9**

With this workaround, I was able to run and debug the latest Functions code in VS2017
---

### Environment summary

**Install Method:** How did you install the CLI? (e.g. pip, interactive script, apt-get, Docker, MSI, nightly)  
Answer here:
Azure Functions Core tools ->NPM 
Azure CLI -> manually

**CLI Version:** What version of the CLI and modules are installed? (Use `az --version`)  
Answer here:
Azure Functions Core Tools (1.0.0-beta.99)
Function Runtime Version: 1.0.11002.0

**OS Version:** What OS and version are you using?  
Answer here: Windows 10 Creator's update

issue_title: Safer ways of deleting notebooks - or undoing delete?
issue_body:
[Picking up on a googlegroups thread: [Disable "Delete Notebooks" option...](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/jupyter/x46rBLUeOHU).]

After mistakenly deleting a bundle of (fortunately) backed up notebooks, I wondered if there might be safer ways of managing this interaction.

If I have currently selected a number a notebooks, clicking the red delete button currently prompts me for confirmation with the message *Are you sure you want to permanently delete the N files/folders selected?*. I'm often blond to these messages, and if for some reason I don't check which notebooks are ticked I could delete many of them by mistake.

One way to reinforce the confirmation message might be to show a list of notebooks to be deleted in the confirmation dialogue, along with pre-ticked checkboxes that show exactly which notebooks are about to be deleted, allowing you to uncheck ones mistakenly included in the deletion list.

Another option identified in the thread was an option to *undo* a delete, with 'wastebin cacheing' enabled for thirty days, for example. (I wonder if the checkpointing mechanism can be appropriated for this, redirected to a wastebin area?)

For the paranoid educator in me, being able to disable or move the delete notebook button somehow (a `custom.js` tweak was suggested in the original thread, but I'm not sure what to disable?) could be a good option.
The requirement here is for Jupyter to be able to run in a Kerberos cluster where the authentication is performed at the cluster Gateway.

issue_title: Inform the user if the zone file on disk does not exist.
issue_body: The `blockstack update` command is able to take both a zone file as a CLI arg, as well as a path to a file on disk with the zone file data.  However, if the path does not exist, then the CLI thinks that the path itself is the literal zone file contents.  This must be fixed--the CLI should be able to detect and resolve this ambiguity (e.g. give a warning if the zone file argument looks like a path, but refers to a non-existent file).

All of us will, of course, start working on 0.16 later on and the plan is more about how the work will be split initially, how we'll deal with maintaining the stable release, and how we'll introduce consensus-breaking features.

Relations with no units still show up in relations list
issue_body:
During the `-departed` hook relations with no units [are removed](https://github.com/juju-solutions/charms.reactive/blob/master/charms/reactive/endpoints.py#L185-L195) from the collection, but during subsequent hooks the empty relation is added back into the collection.

Since the relation technically exists, maybe we should not bother removing it from the collection during `-departed` and just leave it up to the charm author to filter out empty relations if they need to, but I can't really see having access to those empty relations being particularly useful, so maybe it would be nicer to just always filter them out.

As per the comment in [`pluggy._MultiCall`](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pluggy/blob/master/pluggy.py#L586) we should be able to drop the line [here](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pluggy/blob/master/pluggy.py#L613). Grepping the `pytest` sources reveals that only a [few spots still reference it](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=__multicall__).

This change also allows us to remove the recursion induced by [`_wrapped_call()` getting passed `_MultiCall.execute`](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pluggy/blob/master/pluggy.py#L613). issue_title: Test markup needed to categorize all tests according to function
issue_body:
We need an effort in our test suites to markup tests with labels that describe the code under test's functionality. I think the best way for now would be to categorize them from a top-down perspective, starting from the most broad description of the suite to the specific type of test. An example would be a label for all tests under the f5/bigip/tm/sys as 'sys' tests. Another label might be 'negative', where all tests that ensure the code under tests handles fuzzy (or bad) input properly is tested. Of course this all means we have to agree on what is a 'negative' test. 

The idea is that this would allow a developer to make changes a run a particular suite of tests based on label. I might be working on tests against the '11.6.0' Rest API and will naturally want to run those tests as I'm developing.

Note that this doesn't mean that we can only run targeted tests and then call it a day. We still have to make sure all tests pass before being merged in. There's more here to do, but I think starting at the top level might be easiest, rather than labeling one test a 'fuzzy' and having no consensus on what that means.
This API is not yet in the sdk and needs to be added

issue_title: Consumer.get_watermark_offsets Ignores Timeout Parameter in Some Situations
issue_body:
Description
===========
The get_watermark_offsets ignores the timeout and blocks forever when the kafka broker for a selected partition is down. Once the broker is back up, the function returns. I suspect this is a bug in rdkafka, but I'm posting it here first in case it's an issue with the python bindings.

How to reproduce
================
Create a topic with one partition. Run the below example. Once the example has consumed a few messages, kill the broker hosting the partition. The call to get_watermark_offsets will block until the broker comes back up.

```python
import time
import sys
import confluent_kafka
from confluent_kafka import Consumer, KafkaError
from uuid import uuid4

if __name__ == '__main__':
    debug_thread = threading.Thread(target=debug_thread_func)
    debug_thread.start()

    client = Consumer({'bootstrap.servers': 'gateway:9092', 'group.id': str(uuid4()),
              'default.topic.config': {'auto.offset.reset': 'smallest'}})

    def assigned(consumer, partitions):
        print("Assigned:", partitions)
        

    client.subscribe(['ibbot'], on_assign=assigned)

    while True:
        msg = client.poll(timeout=1)
        for partition in client.assignment():
            print(client.get_watermark_offsets(partition, timeout=1))

        if msg is not None:
            if msg.error():
                print("Error: ", msg.error())
            else:
                print("Data")

    client.close()
   

```

Note that this example is probably dependent on a bug in rdkafka, so it may not be reproducible 100% of the time. You can also reproduce this with a multi-partition and broker setup. In this case, the function only blocks for as long as it takes for a new leader to be elected.


Checklist
=========
Please provide the following information:

 - [x] confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka version (`confluent_kafka.version()` and `confluent_kafka.libversion()`):  kafka-python: ('0.11.4', 721920)  rdkafka: ('0.11.4', 722175)
 - [x] Apache Kafka broker version: 0.11.0.2
 - [x] Client configuration: `{'bootstrap.servers': 'gateway:9092', 'group.id': str(uuid4()),
              'default.topic.config': {'auto.offset.reset': 'smallest'}}`
 - [X] Operating system: RHEL 7
 - [ ] Provide client logs (with `'debug': '..'` as necessary)
 - [ ] Provide broker log excerpts
 - [ ] Critical issue

E: pybuild pybuild:274: test: plugin distutils failed with: exit code=1: python2.7 setup.py test 
dh_auto_test: pybuild --test -i python{version} -p 2.7 returned exit code 13
debian/rules:9: recipe for target 'build' failed
make: *** [build] Error 25

In field constructors, validate=validation_function doesn't flow naturally in English.
An average person, before reading the docs, might think that a parameter named "validate" would take a boolean and turn validation on or off, like "strict".

For a single validator, Field(validator=is_lowercase) sounds more natural.
For multiple validators, Field(validators=[is_lowercase, is_alphanumeric]) sounds more natural.
For convenience, these could be aliases of the same method, although I can't think of a case where a developer-user of this API would not know whether they're validating with one or multiple functions.

issue_title: Improve the API design of create_serial_connection
issue_body: The implementation of create_serial_connection currently creates there `Serial` object, forwarding the `*args` and `**kwargs` parameters. A more flexible design would be to allow a `Serial` instance to be passed in by the caller.

issue_title: Z3_goal API breaks invariant I expected to hold
issue_body:
This is a minor issue.

I was playing with Z3's goal API over the weekend and I noticed that an invariant I expected to hold wasn't.

I expected that when I add a formula to a goal (via `Z3_goal_assert()`) the number of formulas in the goal (as reported by `Z3_goal_size()`) to increment by one.

This does not appear to be the case. It seems that the goal eagerly modifies inserted formulas before any tactic is applied to it.

This is what I've observed so far.

* If the inserted formula is a logical `and`  it gets split into separate formula (i.e. the goal size is incremented by `n` for a `n-ary` logical `and`, instead of by 1).
* If `true` is inserted into the goal it is ignored (i.e. increment is 0).
* If `false` is inserted into the goal all existing formulas are removed from the goal and the goal will only contain `false`. Attempting to add more formulas to this goal from now on does nothing.

From the user's perspective I wasn't expecting this to happen. I was expecting Z3 to add the formulas I asked to be inserted into the goal verbatim. I was expecting these changes to be made once a tactic was applied to the goal, not before.

Either the behaviour of `Z3_goal`s needs to be change or it should be documented what `Z3_goal_assert()` will do. My guess is changing the documentation would be the better option.

I think I came across an unsoundness in unstable branch, see this [file](https://gist.github.com/kanigsson/137b4c41d96c97e586c6).

The VC is supposed to be "sat" as far as I can see, and this is what z3 returns on the file as is. But replacing a sort + function definition by a data type definition makes z3 return unsat (see comment at line 51).

I know that a data type is more than just a type with function declarations, but in this VC it shouldn't make a difference.

Is there maybe a bug in z3 with data types?

Thanks for considering this.

Right now our `AppRecord` is basically tied to Electron. @captainsafia and I did some talking about this ~1 week ago (?) and we think we've got three different kinds of `AppRecord`s, some of which have overlap and some of which do not.

## Current `AppRecord`

```
type AppRecordProps = {
  executionState: "not connected" | "busy" | "idle" | "starting",
  token: ?string,
  channels: ?Channels,
  spawn: ?ChildProcess,
  connectionFile: ?string,
  notificationSystem: ?Object,
  kernelSpecName: ?string,
  kernelSpecDisplayName: ?string,
  kernelSpec: ?Object,
  isSaving: boolean,
  lastSaved: ?Date,
  configLastSaved: ?Date,
  error: any
};
```

## `JupyterAppRecord`

Some of the above

* token
* channels
* kernel id
* session id

Intended for `nteract_on_jupyter`

```
class IntRule(TokenRule):

issue_title: Pandas to Postgres -> My own method is a lot faster than odo (or I made a mistake)
issue_body:
I have a pandas dataframe of 8 million rows. I found a fast method on stackoverflow to write this data to Postgres: takes less than 2 minutes. 
Today I found odo and was hoping it would be faster, but it wasn't. 
In fact my guess is it would take 30 minutes or so with odo, I stopped after 7 minutes.

Here's the code for faster writing of dataframe to Postgres, hope it helps you guys:
```
from cStringIO import StringIO

def df_to_database(engine, df, schema, table_name, if_exists='replace', sep='\x01', encoding='utf-8'):
    # Create Table
    df[:0].to_sql(table_name, engine, if_exists=if_exists, index=False, schema=schema)

    # Prepare data
    output = StringIO()
    df.to_csv(output, sep=sep, header=False, encoding=encoding, index=False)
    output.seek(0)

    # Insert data
    connection = engine.raw_connection()
    cursor = connection.cursor()
    schema_tablename = '{}.{}'.format(schema, table_name)
    cursor.copy_from(output, schema_tablename, sep=sep, null='')
    connection.commit()
    cursor.close()
```

issue_title: Add an API for resolving a URL against another
issue_body:
Hi!

`url.click` appears to take a str. It seems like it would be nice to have an API that takes a `URL` instead.

(For the same reasons why dealing with `URL`s and not `str`s is better in the first place :)
#+begin_example

There is a test in the legacy suite (*JobCopyConstructor.gpi*) which looks like:
```python
j = Job()
j2 = j.copy()
j3 = Job(j)

assert j == j2
assert j2 == j3
```
Naïvely I expected this to fail as things like the `id` would have changed but I checked the `Job`'s schema and it has `comparable=0` set on the `id`  (and some other items). 

Looking back at previous results I see that this test was failing in 6.0.44, passing in 6.1.14, still passing in 6.1.13 and failing again in 6.1.14.

The code as written should have this test passing as `GangaObject` equivalence ignores attributes with `comparable=0`. The failure for 6.1.16 is due to `postprocessors` not equating which is a bug which needs looking into.

The larger question is *what do we want object equality to look like?* In my opinion two `GangaObject`s should only be equal if *all* their schema attributes are the same. `__eq__` at the moment seems to be performing an "are these two objects a bit similar" test. If we really want this latter comparison then an `is_similar_to()` function might make sense.

Thoughts?

I wish to apply L2-regularization to the weights of a network. the function add_weight_regularizer seems relevant, but I am not sure how to use it for my purpose. Instead, I define a custom, L2-regularized objective function (described below) according to the conventions in tflearn/objectives.py.

**My question** is whether there is a more simple, straightforward way using add_weight_regularizer or other TFlearn library functions.

This is what I tried:

```
# true iff the given variables is a weight variable
def is_weight(var):
    return var.name.split("/")[1][0] == "W"

# retrive all weight variable in the network
def retrieve_weights():
    trainable_vars = tflearn.variables.get_all_trainable_variable()
    return [var for var in trainable_vars if is_weight(var)]

# calculate the penalty on the norm of the weights and add to the loss
def regularize_loss(loss, weights, beta):
    beta = tf.constant(beta, dtype=tf.float32, name="lambda")
    for weight in weights:
        reg_param = tf.multiply(beta, tf.nn.l2_loss(weight))
        loss = tf.add(loss, reg_param, name=weight.name.split("/")[0])
    return loss

# given the parameter beta (amount of regularization), generate a 
# custom function for regularized categorical-cross-entropy loss
def l2reg_categorical_crossentropy(beta=0.0005):
    def loss_func(y_pred, y_true):
        with tf.name_scope("L2RegCrossentropy"):
            y_pred /= tf.reduce_sum(y_pred,
                                    reduction_indices=len(y_pred.get_shape()) - 1,
                                    keep_dims=True)

            # Manual computation of crossentropy
            y_pred = tf.clip_by_value(y_pred, tf.cast(_EPSILON, dtype=_FLOATX),
                                      tf.cast(1. - _EPSILON, dtype=_FLOATX))
            cross_entropy = - tf.reduce_sum(y_true * tf.log(y_pred),
                                            reduction_indices=len(y_pred.get_shape()) - 1)

            # THE ONLY DIFFERENCE TO categorical_crossentropy IN tflearn/objectives.py*
            weights = retrieve_weights()
            loss = tf.reduce_mean(cross_entropy)
            loss = regularize_loss(loss, weights, beta)
        return loss
    return loss_func
```

Finaly, the new custom loss function is used as follows:

```
loss = utils.l2reg_categorical_crossentropy(beta=0.001)
adam = Adam(learning_rate=0.001, beta1=0.9, beta2=0.999, epsilon=0.1, use_locking=False)
# Model 
network = ...
:
network = regression(network, optimizer=adam, loss=loss)
# Train
...
```

There are a few types of estimators w.r.t. how much data they need to see before "being trained."

- Stateless: No training is required
- Incremental: Can be trained on batches. Additional examples will change the learned parameters
- Full Pass: Must see "all" the data.

Given a pipeline with a mixture of these, we have a choice of how data should flow through the pipeline. With stateless and "full pass" it doesn't really matter; our choice is already made. With an incremental estimator, we can train on the entire dataset before moving to the next estimator, or we can do things in blocks. The "correct" choice will depend on a few things. In an out-of-core context, doing things blockwise will minimize IO, as each block can be loaded and passed through the pipeline.
See https://docs.google.com/document/d/1KgQAarXdQyYBr757NOOLT6rJoitHnope6S83khid7n8/edit#

I was using python2.7 but switched to python3.5 because of issue 29.

This has been spooking around in my head since at least a year. I have no idea how to solve it, but if nothing else, we need to document it.
## Introduction

The vdirsyncer storage API has two operations for writing events/contacts ("items"): `upload` and `update`. 

(The following is a simplified representation that ignores the differences between hrefs and UIDs, and asserts that hrefs are always directly derived from UIDs)
- `upload(item) -> etag`: If an item doesn't already exist with that UID, add `item` to the storage. Otherwise raise an error.
- `update(item, etag) -> new_etag`: If an item with the given `item`'s UID already exists and its etag is `etag`, replace it with `item`. Otherwise raise an error.

All of this is supposed to happen "atomically" (depending on the exact storage type):
- It must be impossible to call `upload(item)` rapidly at the same time with items that have the same UID, and get more than one success (=no errors).
- It must be impossible to call `update(item, etag)` rapidly at the same time with the same etag and get more than one success.

For the storage types `*dav`, `remotestorage`, `google_*`, we basically rely on the server to fullfill this guarantee.
## Problem

`filesystem` storage is tricker in that regard. Assuming that each item is called `<UID>.txt` in the folder `V`:
- `upload`:
  1. Create a temporary file in `V`, write the entire item to it.
  2. Call `link()` to create a second hardlink for the temporary file under the correct filename. This will fail if the target file already exists.
  3. To clean up (both in case of success or error), `unlink` the file from the temporary filename.
  
  Also the temporary file has the file extension `.tmp`, such that it is ignored when listing items, should it be left over for some reason (power loss).
  
  This implementation fullfills the above requirement for `upload`: It is impossible to `link` two files to the same filepath at the same time.
- `update`: Complete trashfire.
  1. Read `etag` from existing file. If the file doesn't exist, error. If the `etag` doesn't match, error.
  2. Create temporary file.
  3. Write to temporary file.
  4. `rename()` temporary filename to correct filename. This removes the previously existing file.
  
  Directly after Step 1, anything can happen. For example, two `update` operations from two clients could happen at the same time. Both clients read the etag, and see that it matches. Then they both overwrite the existing file. Both operations succeed, but only one item actually gets written. The other item is lost.

To be honest I doubt anything of this matters. As far as I can tell, no vdir-software other than vdirsyncer actually checks the etags before writing items.

Awaiting your comments. In hindsight this reads like rambling.

issue_title: Support transport protocols (NSQ, Web socket, HTTP 2.0)
Improve resiliency when ES is not up-to-date
issue_body:
<!-- Provide a general summary of the issue in the Title above -->
When there is an issue related to tasks and ES indexation is lagging behind, we should not crash on latest datasets display.

See: https://sentry.data.gouv.fr/etalab/www-datagouvfr/issues/126047/

Involved pages:

*  https://www.data.gouv.fr/fr/datasets/?sort=-created
* https://www.data.gouv.fr/fr/datasets/?sort=-last_modified

## Possible Implementation
<!-- Not obligatory, but suggest an idea for implementing addition or change -->

Check that the response contains valid datasets, not a list of empty dicts:

`u'datasets': <Response: [{}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}]>`

Otherwise when we call the `url` template tag on empty dicts it crashes.

issue_title: Method and frequency of checking job status.
issue_body:
Checking status of a job is not very easy, basically we need to check if a status file has been updated in the past 5 seconds or so for running jobs, and check the time stamp of task, result, and status files in other cases. A bigger problem is how frequently we should check the status of jobs.

We are checking the status of a job every 2 seconds at the beginning, and at about 30 seconds for long jobs. This does not sound like much but if we have 100 jobs on the cluster,, we are firing 3000 login request to the cluster to check the status of these jobs. This is not acceptable.

issue_title: Lock error with concurrent and undetermined output.
issue_body:
The following code triggers a lock error:
```
input: for_each={'i': range(5)}, concurrent=True
output: dynamic('*.txt')
import random
sh: expand=True
touch {random.randint(0, 10000)}.txt
```
In gitlab by @sylvaingaudan on Apr 28, 2017, 13:34

### Problem

When working through the lang_model.ipynb notebook the following code produced the following error:

```
TEXT = data.Field(lower=True)
FILES = dict(train='wiki.train.tokens', validation='wiki.valid.tokens', test='wiki.test.tokens')
bs,bptt = 80,70
md = LanguageModelData.from_text_files(PATH, TEXT, **FILES, bs=bs, bptt=bptt, min_freq=10)
len(md.trn_dl), md.nt, len(md.trn_ds), len(md.trn_ds[0].text)

em_sz = 200
nh = 500
nl = 3
learner = md.get_model(SGD_Momentum(0.7), em_sz, nh, nl)
reg_fn=partial(seq2seq_reg, alpha=2, beta=1)

clip=0.3
learner.fit(10, 1, wds=1e-6, reg_fn=reg_fn, clip=clip)
```

Error:
```
TypeError: fit() got multiple values for keyword argument 'reg_fn'
```

### Reason For Problem
In `learner.py` in the final call to `fit()` (in `fit_gen()`) both `**kwargs` and `reg_fn` and `clip` get passed to `fit()` which means that if you already specified a `reg_fn` and/or a value for `clip` when calling `learner.fit()` then we end up with duplicate keyword arguments because `reg_fn` and `clip` have already been defined as instance variables of `learner` in the constructor. 

### Proposed Solution
Maybe the API has just evolved so that you always pass `reg_fn` and `clip` to the `learner` constructor. But if the idea is to maintain the ability to pass in different `reg_fn`s and `clip` values at different stages of training then maybe it would be better to check in `fit_gen()` if `reg_fn` or `clip` are already present in the `**kwargs` and if so to pull them out before passing them along to `fit()`.

The `Course.list_multiple_submissions` method returns a paginated list of `Submission` instances.  However, printing any of these instances using its default string conversion fails:

```python
submissions = course.list_multiple_submissions(student_ids='all', assignment_ids=(123, 456))
for submission in submissions:
    print(submission)
```

> `AttributeError: 'Submission' object has no attribute 'id'`

If instead I print each submission’s `__class__` and attribute dictionary, I see something rather strange.  The class is indeed `canvasapi.submission.Submission`.  However, the attributes are not those that a `Submission` should have.  Ignoring the double-underscore internal attributes, each `submission` instance has:

- `_requester`
- `attributes`
- `integration_id`
- `section_id`
- `set_attributes`
- `sis_user_id`
- `submissions`
- `to_json`
- `user_id`

The `sis_user_id` and `user_id` attributes tell me that this is some sort of person-identifying structure along the lines of a `User` or `Enrollment` object.  But it does not have the complete set of attributes for either of those.  The value of the `submissions` attribute is a list of dicts; I believe that each of these dicts corresponds to one `Submission` object.

The `Course.list_multiple_submissions` method produces a `GET /api/v1/courses/:course_id/students/submissions` request, and [the documentation for that request](https://canvas.instructure.com/doc/api/submissions.html#method.submissions_api.for_students) shows that its response can take one of two forms depending on the `grouped` parameter.  I can see that the `Course.list_multiple_submissions` implementation always hard-codes `grouped=False`, but the response coming back looks exactly like the Canvas API documentation example of a *grouped* response, complete with those `submissions` attributes giving lists of `Submission` objects.

So it seems that the `grouped=False` aspect of the request is not working as intended.  I don’t know why; is this a Canvas bug or a `canvasapi` bug?  Either way, the current behavior certainly is not working as intended.  `canvasapi` should either request a non-grouped response in a way that works, or else it should process the grouped response in a way that respects the actual structure and meaning of the returned data.

For now I am working around this myself by building new `Submission` instances out of the dicts in each of the `submissions` attributes, like this:

```python
groups = course.list_multiple_submissions(student_ids='all', assignment_ids=assignmentIds)
submissions = [Submission(course._requester, raw) for raw in chain.from_iterable(group.submissions for group in groups)]
```

But clearly that is not how the API is intended to be used.

issue_title: [V3 CogManager] Paths being added implicitly and incorrectly
issue_body:
# Other bugs

Paths (specifically the core cogs path and the cog install path) are being added to CogManager's config implicitly and incorrectly whenever `CogManager.add_path()` or `CogManager.remove_path()` is called. Basically, the methods get the existing list of paths with `CogManger.paths()`, which returns all valid path directories including the cog install path and the core cogs path. The new/old path is then added/removed from this list and the list is set again.

This is undesirable since:
 1. The install path will stay in the list of valid cog paths even after it's been changed
 2. The core cogs path should only be valid within the scope of a single *install*, not the scope of an *instance* (multiple installs can use one instance)

#### How can we reproduce this issue?

1. Use `[p]addpath` or `[p]removepath` once in any valid way
2. Use `[p]paths` and take note of the install path
3. Use `[p]installpath` to change the install path
4. Use `[p]paths` again and notice that the install path is still in the list of valid paths
5. Also notice that the core cogs path (located at red's install location) is listed as a valid path, even if you change the install location.

span_context_from_string will raise exceptions when  carrier given is not valid.

In http request case, carrier is very likely the request's headers，

thus, upstream user can trigger exceptions by pass an invalid header (like 'uber-trace-id': 'xxx') on purpose.

When running locally, I've noticed that if I try to create a dynamodb table _with_ streams enabled, I get a network timeout, but if the stream is disabled I get a table created right away. Interestingly, the table appears to be created — the NodeJS client is just never informed of it. Steps to Reproduce ================== Start up the container: ```bash docker run -it \ -e SERVICES="dynamodb,dynamodbstreams" \ -p 4569:4569 \ -p 4570:4570 \ -p 8080:8080 \ -e DATA_DIR="/tmp/localstack/data" \ -v ${HOME}/data/localstack:/tmp/localstack \ localstack/localstack ``` Browse to [the web shell](http://localhost:4569/shell/) for DynamoDB. Try to create a DynamoDB table with streams _disabled_: ```javascript var params = { TableName: 'test-without-executions', KeySchema: [ { AttributeName: 'id', KeyType: 'HASH' } ], AttributeDefinitions: [ { AttributeName: 'id', AttributeType: 'S' } ], ProvisionedThroughput: { ReadCapacityUnits: 10, WriteCapacityUnits: 10 }, StreamSpecification: { StreamEnabled: false } }; dynamodb.createTable(params, function(err, data) { if (err) ppJson(err); // an error occurred else ppJson(data); // successful response }); ``` and celebrate, for the table is created! No logs from docker. Next, try to create a _very similar_ table with streams _enabled_: ```javascript var params = { TableName: 'test-with-executions', KeySchema: [ { AttributeName: 'id', KeyType: 'HASH' } ], AttributeDefinitions: [ { AttributeName: 'id', AttributeType: 'S' } ], ProvisionedThroughput: { ReadCapacityUnits: 10, WriteCapacityUnits: 10 }, StreamSpecification: { StreamEnabled: true, StreamViewType: 'NEW_IMAGE' } }; dynamodb.createTable(params, function(err, data) { if (err) ppJson(err); // an error occurred else ppJson(data); // successful response }); ``` and weep - the network console hangs and eventually delivers the following error: Again, no logs from the docker side of things. The table appears to have been created successfully :confused:?

issue_title: Better enrichment of Git and GitHub raw indices to calculate metrics for Manuscripts
issue_body:
Hey all
I've been trying to calculate the GMD metrics and to add them to Manuscripts.
There are some fields and values that I think should be added to the enriched index when it is being created so that it becomes easier to calculate the metrics related to those fields. The metrics can be derived from some other field in the enriched index, but never the less, I think there is a need to produce better enriched indices for Git and GitHub data sources, for now.
There are several Metrics which need additional data in the enriched indices, and they are as follows:

- [Issue Resolution Efficiency](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/issue-resolution-efficiency.md). There is an [issue opened under WG-GMD](https://github.com/chaoss/wg-gmd/issues/5) and as you can see from the discussion, we actually need to figure out if issues have a proposed (and accepted PR) for them to be able to get the title of a successfully closed issue.

- [First Response to Issue Duration](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/first-response-to-issue-duration.md). [Related issue in WG-GMD](https://github.com/chaoss/wg-gmd/issues/8). We may need to calculate the time to first response to the issue.

**NOTE:** I also wanted to put this out there, I think we need to interlink issues and PRs by mentioning the issue number in the proposed PR and vice versa. This way it'll be easier to track down both of them and see the progress.

- [Code Reviews](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/code-reviews.md): as far as I can tell, there is no information about the reviews that are given to a PR. This information needs to be fetched using the API and added into the enriched index.

- [Code Merge Duration](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/code-merge-duration.md) | What is the duration of time between code merge request and code commit?

- [Code Review Efficiency](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/code-review-efficiency.md) | What is the number of merged code changes/number of abandoned code change requests?
- [Maintainer Response to Merge Request Duration](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/maintainer-response-to-merge-request-duration.md) | What is the duration of time for a maintainer to make a first response to a code merge request?
- [Code Review Iteration](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/code-review-iteration.md) | What is the number of iterations that occur before a merge request is accepted or declined?
- [Forks](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/forks.md) | Forks are a concept in distributed version control systems like GitHub. It is a proxy for the approximate number of developers who have taken a shot at building and deploying the codebase *for development*.

- [Pull Request Comment Duration](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/pull-requests-comment-duration.md) | The difference between the timestamp of the pull request creation date and the most recent comment on the pull request.
- [Pull Request Comment Diversity](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/pull-requests-comment-diversity.md) | Number of each people discussing each pull request.
- [Pull Request Comments](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/pull-request-comments.md) | Number of comments on each pull request. 


I think these or fields from which these can be derived need to be added to the enriched indices.

I am also adding issues for the GMD Metrics which need more polishing and definition  under [WG-GMD](https://github.com/chaoss/wg-gmd/issues) repository.
I plan on generating PRs to add the above metrics and this issue can be used as the Parent issue for the same.

**问题**

**代码及截图**

Discuss options for integrating with log-collector-analyzer (https://github.com/oVirt/ovirt-log-collector/blob/master/src/inventory_report/)

Speaking yesterday with @dougsland we reached to two paths:

- ovirt-log-collector-analyzer does spit out json we can consume
- we do implement pre-execution via an 'extension' and we run the sql's individually


First one will be probably the simpler while we don't merge projects together as we could call externally the analyzer if available and report final json with citellus/magui output (also for  being ready to be consumed by other tools)

Second one, even if a bit more complex, as we'll require to implement also a 'cleanup' function for extensions on citellus end of execution will be more unified as we could setup the database they use at init(), report each sql individually and even 'filter' the ones we want to execute or report, and run all the queries in parallel (one per CPU) as we do already now for the other plugins and extensions.

Additionally, for the database location and temporary table we might need to provide extra data for each plugin, which might not be relevant after execution, so I was thinking about filling in listplugins() database access details as created in init() so each plugin will contain those details, and once execution is done in '.run()', remove it as we don't want it to clog the citellus.json.

This will also be related with #423  and some other discussion we had about plugin parameters (which we rejected as we decided to inherit all from environment).
